16.2: A fulfillment: 'YHWH said to Moses, Tell Aaron your brother not to come at any time into the Holy Place inside the veil, before the mercy seat that is on the ark, so that he may not die. For I will appear in the cloud over the mercy seat.' The entire Yom Kippur ritual — its restrictions, its blood-sprinkling, its once-per-year access, its careful approach protocol — is the OT's most detailed type of Christ's high-priestly work. Hebrews 9:1-14 maps the Yom Kippur elements onto Christ systematically: the holy of holies → heaven; the high priest → Christ; bull and goat blood → Christ's own blood; annual repetition → once for all; the mercy seat → Christ as hilasterion (Rom 3:25). Every element of Leviticus 16 is the shadow; Christ is the substance.
16.1: After the death of Aaron's two sons when they drew near before YHWH — Yom Kippur's legislation is framed by the deaths of Nadab and Abihu (Lev 10:1-3): unauthorized approach to the most holy place results in death. This death-framing establishes the theological premise for the entire chapter: the problem of human approach to the holy God. Christ answers this problem not by heightening the restriction (as Yom Kippur's annual once-only limitation does) but by permanently resolving it: 'Therefore, brothers, since we have confidence to enter the holy places by the blood of Jesus' (Heb 10:19).
16.3: Aaron shall come into the Holy Place with a young bull for a sin offering and a ram for a burnt offering — the high priest enters with specific prescribed animals. Hebrews 7:27 identifies the contrast: 'He has no need, like those high priests, to offer sacrifices daily, first for his own sins and then for those of the people, since he did this once for all when he offered up himself.' Aaron's required bull for his own sins is the type that reveals Christ's superiority: the perfect High Priest needs no personal sin offering because he has no sin to atone for.
16.4: He shall put on the holy linen coat and shall have the linen undergarments on his body — on Yom Kippur the high priest strips the gold and jewels and wears only white linen. The voluntary self-reduction to the simplest priestly garments on the day of atonement is the type of Christ's kenosis: 'who, though he was in the form of God, did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped, but emptied himself, taking the form of a servant' (Phil 2:6-7). The glory is not abandoned but veiled; the linen covers the gold as the human nature veils the divine glory.
16.5: From the congregation two male goats for a sin offering — the two-goat complex is a single sin offering with a split function: one goat dies, one carries sin away. Together they accomplish what no single sacrifice mechanism can express: the removal of sin's guilt (through death and blood) and the removal of sin's presence (through the carrying-away to the wilderness). Christ fulfills both simultaneously: 'the Lamb of God who takes away (airōn) the sin of the world' (John 1:29) — takes away by bearing and removing, not merely covering.
16.6: Aaron shall offer the bull as a sin offering for himself — the high priest atones for himself before atoning for the congregation. Christ inverts this sequence: he has no sin to atone for, so his atoning work is entirely on behalf of others from the first moment. 'For we do not have a high priest who is unable to sympathize with our weaknesses, but one who in every respect has been tempted as we are, yet without sin' (Heb 4:15). The absence of a personal sin offering for Christ is the proof of his priestly perfection.
16.7: He shall take the two goats and set them before YHWH at the entrance of the tent of meeting — the two goats presented together as a single offering unit. Christ is the complete fulfillment of both goats simultaneously: his death satisfies divine justice (YHWH-lot goat), and his resurrection carries sin away permanently (Azazel-lot goat). The separation of the two functions in the type is resolved in the single act of the antitype: 'He has appeared once for all at the end of the ages to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself' (Heb 9:26).
16.8: One lot for YHWH and one lot for Azazel — the divine determination of which goat fulfills which function. Christ's appointment as the sacrifice was not contingent or accidental: 'this Jesus, delivered up according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God' (Acts 2:23); 'a lamb without blemish or spot who was foreknown before the foundation of the world' (1 Pet 1:19-20). The lot that falls before YHWH is the type of the eternal election of the Son as both YHWH-lot and Azazel-lot offering.
16.9: The goat on which the lot fell for YHWH shall be used as a sin offering — the slaughtered goat, the one offered to YHWH, provides the blood for the sanctuary's purification. Christ's blood is specifically the purifying agent for the heavenly sanctuary: 'it was necessary for the copies of the heavenly things to be purified with these rites, but the heavenly things themselves with better sacrifices than these' (Heb 9:23). The YHWH-lot goat's blood entering the most holy place is the type of Christ's own blood entering the heavenly sanctuary.
16.10: The goat on which the lot fell for Azazel shall be presented alive before YHWH to make atonement over it — the Azazel goat is presented alive, not yet sent, while Aaron confesses over it. The living goat bears confession before being sent. Christ also lives — is presented before the Father as the living Lord — while his sacrifice accomplished the bearing-away of sin. 'Christ Jesus is the one who died — more than that, who was raised — who is at the right hand of God, who indeed is interceding for us' (Rom 8:34). He is the Azazel-goat still present, alive, interceding.
16.11: Aaron shall present the bull as a sin offering for himself — the second mention of Aaron's personal sin offering. The repetition underlines the contrast with Christ: the Levitical high priest must atone for himself twice (vv.6 and 11); Christ atones for himself zero times. Every mention of Aaron's personal atonement requirement is an implicit pointer to its absence in the perfect High Priest. 'Such a high priest was indeed fitting for us: holy, innocent, unstained, separated from sinners, and exalted above the heavens' (Heb 7:26).
16.12: A censer full of coals from the altar and two handfuls of incense to create a cloud covering the mercy seat — the incense protects Aaron from the unveiled divine presence. Christ enters the heavenly most holy place not with incense for protection but with his own blood that gives him free access: 'by the greater and more perfect tent... not by means of the blood of goats and calves but by means of his own blood, he entered once for all into the holy places, securing an eternal redemption' (Heb 9:11-12). The protection the incense provided is rendered unnecessary by the access Christ's blood secured.
16.13: The incense cloud shall cover the mercy seat so that Aaron does not die — the protective cloud between the priest and the divine presence. Christ needs no such protection: 'he always lives in the presence of God' (Heb 9:24, emfanisthēnai tō prosōpō tou theou — to appear in the presence of God). The Son who is 'the radiance of the glory of God and the exact imprint of his nature' (Heb 1:3) faces the divine presence directly without mediating cloud.
16.14: Some blood of the bull sprinkled on the front of the mercy seat seven times — the blood manipulation on the kapporet. Hebrews 9:7,11-12: 'into the second tent only the high priest goes, and only once a year, and not without blood... Christ entered once for all into the holy places... by means of his own blood, securing an eternal redemption.' Every element of v.14 is explicitly contrasted in Hebrews: Aaron enters with someone else's blood, once a year, for temporary coverage; Christ enters with his own blood, once for all time, for eternal redemption.
16.15: He shall kill the goat of the sin offering that is for the people and bring its blood inside the veil — the communal sin offering's blood entering the most holy place. Christ enters the heavenly sanctuary not with the blood of goats but with his own blood as the communal sin offering for all humanity: 'he is the propitiation for our sins, and not for ours only but also for the sins of the whole world' (1 John 2:2). The communal scope of the Yom Kippur goat's blood — covering all Israel — points to the universal scope of Christ's atoning blood.
16.16: Atonement for the Holy Place because of the uncleannesses of the people — the sanctuary itself requires annual purification from accumulated human impurity. Christ's sacrifice purifies the conscience from dead works: 'how much more will the blood of Christ, who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without blemish to God, purify our conscience from dead works to serve the living God' (Heb 9:14). The sanctuary-purification of the type becomes the conscience-purification of the antitype.
16.17: No one may be in the tent of meeting during the high priest's atoning work — absolute solitude in the most critical priestly moment. In Gethsemane and on the cross, Christ is ultimately alone: 'they all left him and fled' (Mark 14:50); 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' (Mark 15:34). The required solitude of the Yom Kippur high priest is fulfilled in the desolate solitude of the crucified High Priest who bears the full weight of atonement without human support.
16.18: He shall go out to the outer altar and put blood from both offerings on its horns — after the inner sanctuary, the outer altar receives blood from both the priestly and communal sin offerings. The mixed blood (bull + goat) on the altar represents the complete covering of both the priestly and lay dimensions of the community's sin. Christ's blood covers the full spectrum: 'This is my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins' (Matt 26:28). 'Many' (pollōn) translates the Isa 53:12 rabbim — the comprehensive covenant community.
16.19: He shall sprinkle the blood on the outer altar seven times and cleanse and consecrate it — seven sprinklings reconsecrate the altar that has been defiled by the year's accumulated sin. Christ's blood reconsecrates fallen humanity itself: 'you are a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people for his own possession' (1 Pet 2:9). The altar cleansed and reconsecrated by Yom Kippur blood is the type of the royal priesthood cleansed and consecrated by Christ's blood to serve the living God.
16.20: When he has finished atoning for the Holy Place and the tent and the altar, he shall present the live goat — the spatial sequence of atonement ends with the live Azazel goat. After Christ's sacrifice (the YHWH-lot completion), he presents himself alive as the Azazel-dimension: the risen Christ who carries human sin away permanently, seated at the right hand of the Father (Heb 1:3; 10:12). The presentation of the live goat after the atoning work is the type of the resurrection-presentation of the risen Lord who continues to bear and remove sin through his perpetual intercession.
16.21: Aaron shall lay both his hands on the head of the live goat and confess all the iniquities and transgressions and sins of Israel — the most explicit sin-bearing act in the OT, preceding Isa 53's language: the full weight of Israel's sin is transferred to the goat through the double-hand gesture and verbal confession. Isaiah 53:6: 'YHWH has laid on him the iniquity of us all' (hifgea bo, caused to fall upon him). 2 Cor 5:21: 'For our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin.' The Azazel goat's sin-loading is fulfilled in the cross where all human iniquity is laid upon the Son.
16.22: The goat shall bear all their iniquities to a remote area and be let go — nasa (bear, carry, remove) is the verb used for the Servant's sin-bearing in Isa 53:4,11,12: 'he has borne our griefs... he bore the sin of many.' The scapegoat going to the remote cut-off land is the type of Christ's 'descent into the lower parts of the earth' (Eph 4:9), bearing sin into the domain of death and abandonment, and of his permanent removal of sin: 'as far as the east is from the west, so far does he remove our transgressions from us' (Ps 103:12).
16.23: Aaron shall come into the tent of meeting and take off the linen garments he put on when he went into the Holy Place — after the atonement work is complete, the high priest removes the linen and (v.24) resumes the gold vestments. Christ's humiliation is followed by his exaltation: 'therefore God has highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name that is above every name' (Phil 2:9). The resuming of the regular high-priestly garments after Yom Kippur is the type of the resurrection-glorification: the servant-form of the linen gives way to the royal-form of the gold.
16.24: He shall bathe his body in water in a holy place and put on his garments and come out and offer his burnt offering and the burnt offering of the people — the post-atonement purification, change of garments, and resumption of normal priestly ministry. Christ's resurrection is the post-atonement return: he comes out of the tomb having completed the atoning work, resumes his royal-priestly ministry, and presents the burnt offering of his complete consecration to the Father ('it is finished' → the ascension where he presents himself as the final offering, Heb 9:24).
16.25: The fat of the sin offerings burned on the altar — even on Yom Kippur the fat belongs to YHWH. The perpetual fat-offering on the altar on the holiest day of the year confirms that what belongs to God cannot be withheld even in the most intensive human atonement act. Christ's life 'poured out' on the cross (Matt 20:28; Phil 2:17: 'poured out as a drink offering') is the total giving of the divine-human richness to the Father, the ultimate fat-offering on the altar of the cross.
16.26: He who led the goat to Azazel shall wash his clothes and bathe — the sin-laden goat's handler contracts impurity. The disciples who witnessed the crucifixion — who watched the one bearing all human sin — needed the resurrection to make sense of what they had witnessed; the proximity to the sin-bearing Jesus in his death created a kind of moral-spiritual disorientation that only the resurrection-encounter could 'purify.' Peter's reinstatement (John 21:15-19) is the type of the washing-after-scapegoat-handling that the resurrection provided for those who were near the cross.
16.27: The sin offering animals whose blood was brought into the Holy Place shall be burned outside the camp — the outside-camp burning of the most-holy sin offering is the direct type cited in Heb 13:11-13: 'For the bodies of those animals whose blood is brought into the holy places by the high priest as a sacrifice for sin are burned outside the camp. So Jesus also suffered outside the gate in order to sanctify the people through his own blood. Therefore let us go to him outside the camp and bear the reproach he endured.' The type is explicitly applied: Golgotha is the outside-camp burning location of the perfect sin offering.
16.28: He who burns them shall wash his clothes and bathe — the contact with the most holy sin offering carcasses creates impurity for the handler, requiring purification. All who participate in Christ's atoning death — all who are baptized into his death (Rom 6:3) — undergo the washing-and-bathing transformation: 'you were washed, you were sanctified, you were justified in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and by the Spirit of our God' (1 Cor 6:11). The purification required of those who handled the sin offering becomes the washing given to those who come through Christ's death.
16.29: On the tenth day of the seventh month you shall afflict yourselves and do no work — Yom Kippur's communal self-affliction creates corporate participation in the high-priestly atonement; the people join the suffering dimension of the high priest's solitary work through their fast. Christ's call to 'deny yourself and take up your cross' (Mark 8:34) extends the Yom Kippur self-affliction pattern into continuous discipleship: the annual fast becomes the daily self-denial of the new covenant community.
16.30: On this day atonement shall be made for you to cleanse you — mikal chatoteichem lifney YHWH titharu, from all your sins before YHWH you shall be clean. The comprehensive atonement of Yom Kippur — 'all your sins' — is the type of the total justification provided by Christ: 'there is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus' (Rom 8:1). The annual declaration of total cleanliness becomes the permanent verdict of justification in Christ: 'God presented him as the one who would turn aside his wrath, taking away sin' (Rom 3:25 NIV).
16.31: Shabbat shabbaton — the most intense Sabbath rest, the Sabbath of Sabbaths. Yom Kippur's super-Sabbath is the type of the rest secured by Christ's completed atonement: 'whoever has entered God's rest has also rested from his works as God did from his' (Heb 4:10). Christ's 'It is finished' (John 19:30) is the declaration of the ultimate shabbat shabbaton — the rest from the works of atonement, the completed work that creates the basis for the eschatological Sabbath-rest of the people of God.
16.32: The anointed priest who serves in his father's place shall perform the atonement — the successor high priest performs the identical ceremony; the rite is perpetuated through the Aaronic succession. Christ's priesthood is not succeeded but permanent: 'he holds his priesthood permanently, because he continues forever' (Heb 7:24). The Levitical succession that required each generation to repeat the atonement is contrasted with the permanent, non-successive priesthood of the Son whose single atonement requires no successor and no repetition.
16.33: He shall make atonement for the holy sanctuary, the tent, the altar, the priests, and all the people — the comprehensive four-part atonement: sacred spaces and sacred persons. Christ's atonement covers the full scope of what Yom Kippur addressed and more: 'it was necessary for the copies of the heavenly things to be purified with these rites, but the heavenly things themselves with better sacrifices' (Heb 9:23). The range of what the Levitical atonement covered is precisely the range of what Christ's sacrifice definitively covers — and the heavenly originals beyond.
16.34: This shall be a statute forever: once a year atonement shall be made for all the sins of Israel — achat bashanah, once per year. The once-per-year restriction is Hebrews' primary evidence for the inadequacy of the Levitical system and the necessity of Christ's once-for-all sacrifice: 'But when Christ appeared as a high priest of the good things that have come... he entered once for all into the holy places, not by means of the blood of goats and calves but by means of his own blood, securing an eternal redemption' (Heb 9:11-12). Nor is it repeated annually any longer: 'he has appeared once for all at the end of the ages to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself' (Heb 9:26).

17.11: A direct revelation: 'For the life of the flesh is in the blood, and I have given it for you on the altar to make atonement for your souls, for it is the blood that makes atonement by the life.' This verse is the theological axiom that explains why Christ had to die a bloody death — not a natural death, not a symbolic death, but a sacrificial death involving the shedding of blood. The divine appointment ('I have given it for you on the altar') means that blood-atonement is not a primitive accommodation to human psychology but a divine mechanism built into creation-order: life given for life, blood for blood. Christ's death fulfills this principle at its ultimate level: his blood, the blood of the God-man, atones not for one year or one nation but once for all humanity (Heb 9:12).
17.1: YHWH spoke to Moses — the centralization law of ch 17 is covenant-foundational: all slaughter of sacrificial animals must come to the tabernacle. The exclusivity principle encoded here points to Christ's exclusive claim: 'I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me' (John 14:6). Just as all valid sacrifice must go to the one sanctuary, all access to the Father goes through the one Mediator.
17.2: Speak to Aaron and his sons and to all the people — the double address (priestly and lay) of the blood legislation. Christ's sacrifice establishes the same universal standard: his blood is not a priestly prerogative but the universal atonement available to all: 'there is one God, and there is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus, who gave himself as a ransom for all' (1 Tim 2:5-6).
17.3: If any one of the house of Israel kills an ox or a lamb or a goat in the camp or outside the camp — all slaughter of these species anywhere must be brought to the sanctuary. The prohibition of unauthorized sacrifice reflects the principle that human beings cannot generate their own access to God; the sacrifice must come through the divinely appointed place and priest. Christ is 'the one mediator' precisely because all human attempts to approach God outside of his mediation are, in the type's language, unauthorized field slaughter to goat-demons.
17.4: And does not bring it to the entrance of the tent of meeting — bloodguilt for unauthorized sacrifice incurred as though human blood were shed. The severity — treating animal sacrifice outside the sanctuary as equivalent to murder — reflects that unauthorized appropriation of the atoning mechanism is a capital offense against the sovereignty of the one who appointed it. All human-devised 'atonement' outside of Christ's appointed sacrifice incurs the same kind of illegitimacy.
17.5: That the people may bring their sacrifices to YHWH at the entrance of the tent of meeting — the positive purpose of the centralization law: all sacrifice becomes a genuine encounter with YHWH rather than unauthorized deal-making with other powers. The gathering of all worship to the one sanctuary is the type of all worship gathered to the one risen Lord: 'in him all things hold together' (Col 1:17); 'all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me' (Matt 28:18).
17.6: The priest shall throw the blood on the altar and burn the fat for a pleasing aroma to YHWH — the re-channeled field slaughter becomes legitimate peace offering with the standard blood-fat ritual. What was illegitimate outside becomes legitimate when brought to the appointed place and mediator. Christ's mediation transforms unauthorized human grasping into legitimate covenant approach: 'through him we both have access in one Spirit to the Father' (Eph 2:18).
17.7: No more sacrificing to the goat demons after whom they whore — the se'irim (goat-demons, wilderness spirits) represent all alternative spiritual authorities. Christ's cross disarms every competing spiritual power: 'he disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him' (Col 2:15). The prohibition on se'irim sacrifice is fulfilled in Christ's victory over the 'rulers and authorities' who are the NT counterpart to the se'irim of the wilderness.
17.8: Any one of the house of Israel or of the strangers who sojourns among them — the universal scope of the centralization law includes the resident Gentile. Christ's sacrifice is explicitly for all peoples: 'the Lamb who was slain' who 'ransomed people for God from every tribe and language and people and nation' (Rev 5:9). The inclusion of the sojourner in the blood legislation is the type of the inclusion of the Gentiles in the new covenant atonement.
17.9: Who offers a burnt offering or sacrifice and does not bring it to the entrance of the tent — karet for unauthorized sacrifice by Israelite or sojourner. The exclusivity of the one sanctuary prepares for the exclusivity of the one sacrifice. Christ is 'the stone the builders rejected' who has become 'the cornerstone' and about whom Peter declares: 'there is salvation in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved' (Acts 4:11-12).
17.10: If any one eats any blood, I will set my face against that person and will cut him off — the divine first-person threat against blood consumption. Yet Christ commands precisely this: 'unless you eat the flesh of the Son of Man and drink his blood, you have no life in you' (John 6:53). The apparent reversal is resolved by recognizing that the blood prohibition was in force precisely because blood was reserved for the atonement — and when Christ gives his blood as the final atonement, the 'drinking' of his blood is the receiving of that atoning gift. The prohibition protects the type until the antitype arrives and transforms the prohibition into invitation.
17.12: No person among you shall eat blood, and the stranger who sojourns among you shall not eat blood — the universal blood prohibition for all in Israel. The prohibition's universality is the mirror of the universal invitation in Christ: 'Come, everyone who thirsts, come to the waters' (Isa 55:1). The universal prohibition that guarded the blood-as-nefesh for the atonement becomes the universal invitation when the blood-as-nefesh of Christ is offered for all.
17.13: Any one who takes a beast or bird that may be eaten shall pour out its blood and cover it with earth — the earth-covering of game blood. Christ's blood was not merely shed but 'covered' — buried: 'I delivered to you as of first importance... that Christ died for our sins in accordance with the Scriptures, that he was buried' (1 Cor 15:3-4). The burial is an integral part of the atonement sequence, not merely a disposal; the earth that covered the game's blood is the type of the tomb that 'covered' the blood of the Lamb until the resurrection reversed the covering.
17.14: For the life of every creature is its blood: its blood is its life — the nefesh-blood identification restated for all creatures. The life of Christ — his divine-human nefesh — is uniquely present in his blood: 'This cup that is poured out for you is the new covenant in my blood' (Luke 22:20). The life of the Son of God poured out in death is the ultimate fulfillment of the nefesh-in-blood principle: the blood that atones does so precisely because it carries the infinite life of the eternal Son.
17.15: Whoever eats what died of itself or what was torn by beasts shall be unclean until evening — neveilah and terefah (animals that died without proper blood-draining) create impurity because the blood remained in them. The proper draining of blood at slaughter is the type of the proper 'pouring out' of Christ's blood in the sacrifice: 'he poured out his soul to death' (Isa 53:12, heereh lammavet nafsho). Christ is neither neveilah (died of itself) nor terefah (torn randomly) but the appointed sacrificial lamb whose blood was properly given at the appointed time.
17.16: If he does not wash them or bathe, he shall bear his iniquity — the purification requirement after eating improperly-bled meat. The principle that proximity to death (even animal death) requires purification points to the radical purification that proximity to Christ's death accomplishes: 'Do you not know that all of us who have been baptized into Christ Jesus were baptized into his death? We were buried therefore with him by baptism into death, in order that, just as Christ was raised from the dead by the glory of the Father, we too might walk in newness of life' (Rom 6:3-4). The laundering-and-bathing of the type becomes the baptismal dying-and-rising of the antitype.

19.18: A direct revelation: 'You shall love your neighbor as yourself: I am YHWH.' Jesus elevates Lev 19:18 to the second-greatest commandment (Matt 22:39) and Paul calls it the fulfillment of the whole law (Gal 5:14; Rom 13:9). But Christ does not merely cite the command — he embodies it: 'Greater love has no one than this, that someone lay down his life for his friends' (John 15:13). The neighbor-love of Lev 19:18 reaches its ultimate expression in the cross, where Christ loved his neighbors — his enemies — at the cost of his life (Rom 5:8: God demonstrates his love for us in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us).
19.1: A literary frame: the formula 'YHWH spoke to Moses, saying' opens the Holiness Code (chs. 17-26), the dense heart of Leviticus. Every commandment that follows is YHWH's direct speech — the law is not a human ethical system but the expression of the divine character. Christ as the eternal Word (John 1:1) is the personal embodiment of that character: 'the law was given through Moses; grace and truth came through Jesus Christ' (John 1:17). The Holiness Code's 'I am YHWH' refrain (occurring ~15 times in ch. 19 alone) grounds every obligation in the divine self-disclosure that reaches its fullness in the incarnate Son.
19.2: A direct quotation: 'You shall be holy, for I YHWH your God am holy.' This is the Holiness Code's governing mandate, and 1 Peter 1:15-16 quotes it directly as the standard for the new covenant community: 'as he who called you is holy, you also be holy in all your conduct.' The ground of the command shifts in 1 Peter: it is the Father who calls, but the means is 'the precious blood of Christ, a lamb without blemish or spot' (1 Pet 1:19). Christ himself is YHWH's holiness made visible — 'holy, innocent, unstained, separated from sinners, and exalted above the heavens' (Heb 7:26); his people are holy by union with him.
19.3: A shadow: 'Every one of you shall revere his mother and his father, and keep my sabbaths.' Christ himself lived this commandment — twelve years old in the temple yet returning to Nazareth to be subject to Mary and Joseph (Luke 2:51). Yet he also reorders the honor-command in light of ultimate allegiance: 'Whoever loves father or mother more than me is not worthy of me' (Matt 10:37). In Christ the fifth commandment reaches its telos — not the abolition of family honor but its relativization within the kingdom family, where the deepest bond is to the Father and to brothers and sisters in Christ.
19.4: A shadow: 'Do not turn to idols or make for yourselves cast metal images of gods.' Christ is 'the image of the invisible God' (Col 1:15) — the one in whom humanity can truly see God without distortion. The prohibition of cast images is the negative space pointing toward the positive reality: the invisible God, unable to be represented in wood or metal, chose to represent himself in the flesh of the Son. 'Whoever has seen me has seen the Father' (John 14:9). The idol-prohibition is the shadow of the incarnation — no human-made image will suffice; only the image God himself made in his Son.
19.5: A shadow: the fellowship offering regulations open with the requirement that the sacrifice be offered 'for your acceptance' (lirtsonchem) — offered in the way that will be pleasing to YHWH. Christ's offering is the perfect fulfillment: 'he gave himself up for us, a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God' (Eph 5:2). The phrase 'fragrant aroma' (reyach nichoach) applied to the fellowship offering becomes Paul's description of Christ's death — the self-offering that is maximally pleasing to the Father, the sacrifice YHWH accepts in the fullest sense.
19.6: A shadow: flesh left from the fellowship offering past the second day 'shall be burned with fire; it is tainted (pigul).' The time-limit on eating the sacrifice means the celebration of restored fellowship has its appointed season — it cannot be stored up indefinitely. Christ's resurrection breaks the decay-pattern: 'you will not allow your Holy One to see corruption' (Ps 16:10; Acts 2:27). The fellowship meal in Christ is not time-limited; his body does not decay, and the eucharistic feast he inaugurates will find its fullness at the messianic banquet in the age to come.
19.7: A shadow: the tainted offering eaten on the third day shall not be accepted. The number three echoes in the resurrection narrative: 'after three days he will rise' (Matt 27:63). The rejected offering that is eaten beyond its proper time is not accepted — by contrast, Christ's offering, consumed and completed within the proper time (he rose on the third day, not after), is maximally accepted. The sacrifice that fulfilled its purpose in its proper moment is the antithesis of the pigul — the offering YHWH accepted and raised.
19.8: A shadow: eating the tainted offering means 'that person shall bear his iniquity.' The phrase nasa avon (bear iniquity) is the same language used of the suffering servant: 'he bore the sin of many' (Isa 53:12). What the offerer incurs by mishandling the sacrifice, Christ incurred on behalf of all who deserved exclusion — he bore the iniquity that the covenant system threatened to lay on violators, taking it onto himself so that those who come to him 'will certainly not be cast out' (John 6:37).
19.9: A shadow: 'When you reap the harvest of your land, you shall not reap your field to the very edge, and you shall not gather the gleanings after your harvest.' The gleaning law (Lev 19:9-10) is the legal provision for the poor and the alien — deliberate incompleteness as an act of structured generosity. Ruth gleans in Boaz's field under this provision (Ruth 2:2-3), and Boaz's generosity beyond the legal minimum prefigures Christ. Jesus feeds five thousand in the wilderness (John 6:1-14) — abundance with leftovers — and calls the church to share its resources so that 'there was not a needy person among them' (Acts 4:34).
19.10: A shadow: 'You shall not strip your vineyard bare, neither shall you gather the fallen grapes of your vineyard. You shall leave them for the poor and for the sojourner.' The vineyard-gleaning law sets the scene for Jesus's parable of the vineyard (Matt 21:33-43) — the owner who plants a vineyard and expects fruit from the tenants. The messianic vineyard motif runs from Isaiah 5 through John 15 ('I am the true vine'). Christ is the true vine owner who does not strip the vineyard bare but invites the poor and the alien into the harvest of the kingdom.
19.11: A shadow: 'You shall not steal; you shall not deal falsely; you shall not lie to one another.' Christ himself is 'the truth' (John 14:6) — the incarnate anti-lie, the one in whom there is no falsehood (1 Pet 2:22: 'no deceit was found in his mouth'). He fulfills the prohibition of deception not merely by observing the law but by being the ontological ground of truth. He also calls his followers to truthfulness: 'Let what you say be simply Yes or No' (Matt 5:37). The kingdom he inaugurates is a community of truth because its head is the truth himself.
19.12: A shadow: 'You shall not swear by my name falsely, and so profane the name of your God.' Jesus intensifies this command: 'Do not take an oath at all... let what you say be Yes or No' (Matt 5:34-37). Christ himself speaks with direct authority — 'Truly, truly I say to you' (Amen, amen legō hymin) rather than swearing by YHWH, because he is the one through whom YHWH speaks. He is 'the Amen, the faithful and true witness' (Rev 3:14) — the one whose word needs no oath to validate it, who embodies the trustworthiness the oath-law was designed to protect.
19.13: A shadow: 'The wages of a hired worker shall not remain with you all night until the morning.' The immediate payment law is the legal protection for the day-laborer — delay in wages is economic oppression. James 5:4 applies this directly: 'the wages of the laborers who mowed your fields, which you kept back by fraud, are crying out against you, and the cries of the harvesters have reached the ears of the Lord of hosts.' Christ identifies with the oppressed: 'I was hungry and you gave me food... as you did it to one of the least of these my brothers, you did it to me' (Matt 25:35-40). The eschatological judge who hears the cries of the unpaid worker is the same Christ who himself labored for others.
19.14: A shadow: 'You shall not curse the deaf or put a stumbling block before the blind, but you shall fear your God.' Christ reverses the exclusion and danger that disability creates: he opens the ears of the deaf (Mark 7:32-35), gives sight to the blind (John 9), calls the crippled and lame to the messianic banquet (Luke 14:13). The law prohibited making the situation of the disabled worse; Christ makes it radically better. The law's protective intent points toward the kingdom where 'the blind receive their sight and the lame walk' (Matt 11:5) — the messianic signs of the new age.
19.15: A shadow: 'You shall do no injustice in court. You shall not be partial to the poor or defer to the great, but in righteousness shall you judge your neighbor.' Christ as the righteous judge fulfills this perfectly: 'I can do nothing on my own. As I hear, I judge, and my judgment is just, because I seek not my own will but the will of him who sent me' (John 5:30). He was himself subjected to the very injustice Lev 19:15 forbids — the chief priests and elders 'sought false testimony against Jesus' (Matt 26:59), preferring the great (Roman power, temple establishment) over justice. The unjust trial of Christ exposes the law's violation; his resurrection is the Father's just verdict.
19.16: A shadow: 'You shall not go around as a slanderer among your people, and you shall not stand up against the life of your neighbor.' The prohibition against standing by while blood is shed becomes the negative type of Christ's positive act: 'Greater love has no one than this, that someone lay down his life for his friends' (John 15:13). Christ does not stand by passively when his people face death — he intervenes, and the intervention costs him his own life. The law forbids indifference; the gospel shows the ultimate opposite of indifference: a God who enters the danger himself.
19.17: A shadow: 'You shall not hate your brother in your heart, but you shall reason frankly with your neighbor, lest you incur sin because of him.' Jesus builds on this: 'If your brother sins against you, go and tell him his fault, between you and him alone' (Matt 18:15) — the private rebuke as the first step of church discipline. Christ himself enacts this principle: he does not destroy sinners silently but calls them by name ('Zacchaeus, hurry and come down'), confronting sin in order to restore. The law requires honest confrontation; Christ models it as the basis of redemptive community.
19.19: A shadow: 'You shall keep my statutes. You shall not let your cattle breed with a different kind. You shall not sow your field with two kinds of seed, nor shall you wear a garment of cloth made of two kinds of material.' The kilayim (mixed-kinds) laws preserve created distinctions — each according to its kind, the created order of Gen 1. Paul applies the principle typologically: 'Do not be unequally yoked with unbelievers' (2 Cor 6:14) and develops a theology of the new creation community as distinct from the surrounding world. Christ himself is the one who draws the boundary: 'I have chosen you out of the world, therefore the world hates you' (John 15:19). The new covenant community is, like the holy garment and the unmingled field, a distinct kind.
19.20: A shadow: the law concerning a man who lies with a female slave betrothed to another — she shall not be put to death because she was not free; he brings a guilt offering. The gradations of culpability and the provision of atonement even in complex cases of sin point to the principle Christ embodies: no sin is beyond the reach of the guilt offering. Every case, however morally tangled, has a path to atonement. Christ is the guilt offering for the morally complex: 'he who did not know sin was made sin for us' (2 Cor 5:21), covering cases the Levitical system could not fully resolve.
19.21: A shadow: 'He shall bring his guilt offering to YHWH, to the entrance of the tent of meeting, a ram for a guilt offering.' The guilty party must come to the sanctuary to find atonement — the sin cannot be dealt with at a distance. Christ comes to where the sinner is: 'the Word became flesh and dwelt among us' (John 1:14). But then he also brings the sinner to where God is: 'through him we have access in one Spirit to the Father' (Eph 2:18). The guilt offering's movement (sinner to sanctuary) is fulfilled in the double movement of the incarnation (God to sinner) and the ascension (sinner to God through Christ).
19.22: A shadow: 'And the priest shall make atonement for him before YHWH with the ram of the guilt offering for his sin that he has committed, and he shall be forgiven for the sin that he has committed.' The priestly mediation of forgiveness — the priest acts, the sacrifice is accepted, the sinner is forgiven — is the OT's enacted model of what Christ accomplishes once for all. 'He is the propitiation for our sins, and not for ours only but also for the sins of the whole world' (1 John 2:2). The Levitical priest's mediation was real but limited to individual cases; Christ's mediation is universal and permanent.
19.23: A shadow: 'When you come into the land and plant any kind of tree for food, then you shall regard its fruit as forbidden. Three years it shall be forbidden to you; it must not be eaten.' The three-year prohibition before the fruit may be eaten patterns the waiting period before the harvest — appropriate time before the gift is received. The number resonates: Christ's public ministry lasted approximately three years before the fruit of his sacrifice was given. The waiting before the harvest is the shadow of the patience required in the redemptive plan: 'when the fullness of time had come, God sent forth his Son' (Gal 4:4).
19.24: A shadow: 'And in the fourth year all its fruit shall be holy, an offering of praise to YHWH.' The fourth-year fruit is consecrated to YHWH — the harvest dedicated before being enjoyed. Christ rises on the first day of the new week, the day of firstfruits — his resurrection-harvest is first consecrated to the Father ('I am ascending to my Father and your Father, to my God and your God', John 20:17) before becoming the basis for the church's new life. The fourth-year consecration points to the resurrection dedication: Christ offered to the Father before being given to the church.
19.25: A shadow: 'And in the fifth year you may eat of its fruit, to increase its yield for you.' Only after dedication may the full harvest be enjoyed. Christ's gift to the church — the Spirit, the fullness of new covenant life — comes only after his consecration to the Father: 'Nevertheless, I tell you the truth: it is to your advantage that I go away, for if I do not go away, the Helper will not come to you' (John 16:7). The fifth-year abundance follows the fourth-year dedication; the Spirit's abundance in the church follows Christ's ascension and intercession at the Father's right hand.
19.26: A shadow: 'You shall not eat any flesh with blood in it. You shall not interpret omens or tell fortunes.' The prohibition of divination is the law's witness that the future belongs to YHWH alone. Christ fulfills this by being the one through whom God speaks his final word: 'Long ago, at many times and in many ways, God spoke to our fathers by the prophets, but in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son' (Heb 1:1-2). The need for oracles and omens ends when the Word himself comes. Christ also expels the unclean spirits that drove the divination practices of the ancient world: 'the prince of this world has been judged' (John 16:11).
19.27: A shadow: 'You shall not round off the hair on your temples or mar the edges of your beard.' These prohibitions ban the hair-cutting rites associated with pagan mourning practices — the body was not to be ritually disfigured as if death had ultimate power. Christ transforms mourning: 'Blessed are those who mourn, for they shall be comforted' (Matt 5:4). His resurrection means death no longer has the last word, and the mourning-rites that expressed hopeless grief before pagan gods are replaced by the hope of the resurrection. 'I am the resurrection and the life' (John 11:25) — the one in whose presence pagan death-mourning is rendered obsolete.
19.28: A shadow: 'You shall not make any cuts on your body for the dead or tattoo yourselves.' The prohibition against marking the body for the dead has an implicit NT counterpart: 'Do you not know that your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit within you, whom you have from God? You are not your own, for you were bought with a price' (1 Cor 6:19-20). The body belongs to God — in the OT because YHWH is Creator, in the NT because Christ's blood purchased it. The body is not a surface for ritual marking in the service of the dead; it is the Spirit's dwelling, purchased by the living Christ.
19.29: A shadow: 'Do not profane your daughter by making her a prostitute, lest the land fall into prostitution and the land become full of depravity.' The prohibition of sexual exploitation protects the daughter's honor. In Christ's fulfillment, the church is the daughter/bride who must be kept pure: 'I betrothed you to one husband, to present you as a pure virgin to Christ' (2 Cor 11:2). Christ 'gave himself up for her, that he might sanctify her, having cleansed her by the washing of water with the word, so that he might present the church to himself in splendor, without spot or wrinkle' (Eph 5:25-27). The law protects the daughter from prostitution; Christ dies to purify the bride.
19.30: A shadow: 'You shall keep my sabbaths and reverence my sanctuary: I am YHWH.' The sabbath and sanctuary command is fulfilled and transformed in Christ. Christ is 'Lord of the Sabbath' (Matt 12:8), the one who gives true rest: 'Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest' (Matt 11:28). The sanctuary (temple) is his body (John 2:21) and then the community of his Spirit (1 Cor 3:16). The reverence due the physical sanctuary is now the reverence due the risen Christ who is present among his gathered people: 'where two or three are gathered in my name, there am I among them' (Matt 18:20).
19.31: A shadow: 'Do not turn to mediums or necromancers; do not seek them out, and so make yourselves unclean by them.' The prohibition of consulting the dead for guidance is abolished in Christ, who is the one mediator: 'there is one God, and there is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus' (1 Tim 2:5). All spiritual guidance comes through him and by his Spirit: 'When the Spirit of truth comes, he will guide you into all the truth' (John 16:13). The Witch of Endor consulted a dead spirit in despair (1 Sam 28); Christ himself is the living Lord who speaks to his people and gives them no cause to seek the dead.
19.32: A shadow: 'You shall stand up before the gray head and honor the face of an old man, and you shall fear your God.' The honor of the aged reflects YHWH's own eternity — wisdom accumulated over time reflects the character of the God who is from everlasting. Christ is the one in whom all wisdom and knowledge are hidden (Col 2:3) — not wisdom accumulated over time but wisdom that is eternal: 'before Abraham was, I am' (John 8:58). He is 'the Ancient of Days' (Dan 7:9-13) in human form, the one before whom every knee will bow (Phil 2:10) — the ultimate elder deserving honor.
19.33: A shadow: 'When a stranger sojourns with you in your land, you shall not do him wrong.' The protection of the alien is the ethical application of Israel's own experience: 'you were aliens in the land of Egypt.' Christ came to the alienated and the outsider as the one who was himself an outsider: 'the Word... came to his own, and his own people did not receive him' (John 1:11). The alien-protection law finds its fullest expression in the Gentile mission: 'the Gentiles, who did not pursue righteousness, have attained it' (Rom 9:30). The stranger who sojourns is now welcomed into the covenant family through Christ.
19.34: A direct quotation: 'You shall treat the stranger who sojourns with you as the native among you, and you shall love him as yourself, for you were strangers in the land of Egypt.' This verse extends the neighbor-love command of v. 18 explicitly to the alien — the love-your-neighbor command is not restricted to coethnics. Paul builds the entire Gentile-mission theology on this expansion: 'there is no distinction between Jew and Greek; for the same Lord is Lord of all' (Rom 10:12). Christ's death removes 'the dividing wall of hostility' (Eph 2:14) — the once-alien Gentiles are 'no longer strangers and aliens, but fellow citizens with the saints' (Eph 2:19).
19.35: A shadow: 'You shall do no wrong in judgment, in measures of length or weight or quantity.' Honest commercial dealings reflect YHWH's justice — a just God demands just measures from his people. Christ as the coming judge who will evaluate every weight and measure: 'with the judgment you pronounce you will be judged, and with the measure you use it will be measured to you' (Matt 7:2). The eschatological judgment (Rev 6:5-6 — the scales of judgment) renders every dishonest commercial transaction accountable. The just Judge who will weigh all things is the one whose own life was weighed and found to be of infinite worth.
19.36: A shadow: 'You shall have just balances, just weights, a just ephah, and a just hin.' The specific instruments of measurement — the stone weights, the dry measure, the liquid measure — are all subject to YHWH's justice. Christ is the 'faithful and true witness' (Rev 3:14) and the 'cornerstone' (Eph 2:20) — the standard by which all things are measured. The church, called to honest measurement in commerce, is also called to 'speaking the truth in love' (Eph 4:15) — measuring all things by the standard of the one who is truth itself. 'Test everything; hold fast what is good' (1 Thess 5:21).
19.37: A summary: 'You shall observe all my statutes and all my rules and do them: I am YHWH.' The Holiness Code closes with the inclusive summary — all the laws, both civil and religious, point to YHWH's character. Christ's declaration 'Do not think that I have come to abolish the Law or the Prophets; I have not come to abolish them but to fulfill them' (Matt 5:17) is the NT's direct response to Lev 19:37. He fulfills by embodying: the love-of-neighbor, the honest weights, the care for the alien, the purity of worship, the justice of judgment — Christ is the living summary of every statute in chapter 19.

1.1: A revelation of God: YHWH speaks to Moses from within the tent of meeting — the completed tabernacle has become the place of divine speech. The God who commands sacrifice is the God who will himself provide the ultimate sacrifice; the instructions now given from within the sanctuary point forward to the one who will enter the greater sanctuary not made with hands (Heb 9:11).
1.2: A shadow: 'Speak to the people of Israel and say to them, When any one of you brings an offering to YHWH, you shall bring your offering of livestock from the herd or from the flock.' The entire Levitical offering system is a comprehensive shadow-structure. Hebrews calls the law 'a shadow of the good things to come' (Heb 10:1); what is outlined in exhaustive detail in Leviticus 1–7 is the shadow of the one sacrifice of Christ.
1.3: A direct type: 'If his offering is a burnt offering from the herd, he shall offer a male without blemish [tamim].' The requirement of tamim (complete, without defect) in the sacrificial animal is the OT's most persistent type of Christ's sinlessness. 1 Peter 1:18–19 makes the connection explicit: 'You were ransomed... with the precious blood of Christ, like that of a lamb without blemish [amōmos] or spot.' Hebrews 9:14 similarly: 'Christ... offered himself without blemish to God.' Every animal rejected for defect announced that only a perfect substitute could make atonement.
1.4: A direct type: 'He shall lay his hand on the head of the burnt offering, and it shall be accepted for him to make atonement for him.' The hand-laying rite (semichah) is the sacrificial system's most explicit act of identification and substitution — the offerer transfers his own identity onto the animal, which then stands in his place. The NT applies this substitutionary logic to Christ: 'God made him who had no sin to be sin for us' (2 Cor 5:21); 'He himself bore our sins in his body on the tree' (1 Pet 2:24). The hand-press of semichah becomes the imputation of the believer's sin to Christ.
1.5: A direct type: 'Then he shall kill the bull before YHWH, and Aaron's sons the priests shall bring the blood and throw the blood against the sides of the altar.' Blood thrown against the altar is the essential sacrificial act — Hebrews 9:22: 'Without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness of sins.' The requirement that blood be actively presented (not merely spilled) reflects the deliberate, purposeful nature of atonement — and points to Christ's deliberate, voluntary death (John 10:17–18: 'I lay it down of my own accord').
1.6: A shadow: 'And he shall flay the burnt offering and cut it into pieces.' The complete dismemberment and examination of the offering ensures that nothing is concealed or hidden — the sacrifice must be transparent, without hidden defects. Christ submitted to complete exposure: interrogated, stripped, examined publicly — 'this is the judgment, that the light has come into the world' (John 3:19).
1.7: A shadow: 'The sons of Aaron the priest shall put fire on the altar and arrange wood on the fire.' The altar fire was kept continuously burning (Lev 6:12–13, 'the fire on the altar shall be kept burning; it shall not go out'). This perpetual fire is the image of God's consuming holiness — the fire into which Christ entered: 'our God is a consuming fire' (Heb 12:29). The cross was not an accident of history but a divine altar-fire prepared before creation (Rev 13:8).
1.8: A shadow: 'And Aaron's sons the priests shall arrange the pieces, the head, and the fat, on the wood that is on the fire on the altar.' Every part of the sacrifice is carefully arranged — nothing is left off the altar. Christ's offering was total: body, soul, and spirit fully given. 'I give my flesh for the life of the world' (John 6:51) — and more than flesh: 'He offered himself without blemish to God' (Heb 9:14).
1.9: A direct type: 'Its entrails and its legs he shall wash with water. And the priest shall burn all of it on the altar, as a burnt offering, a food offering with a pleasing aroma [reah nichoach] to YHWH.' The olah (burnt offering) is entirely consumed — the word means 'ascending'; every part rises to God as smoke. This total consumption is the type of Christ's total self-giving: 'He loved me and gave himself for me' (Gal 2:20). Paul describes Christ's sacrifice using the reah nichoach formula exactly: 'a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God' (Eph 5:2).
1.10: A direct type: 'If his gift for a burnt offering is from the flock, from the sheep or goats, he shall bring a male without blemish [tamim].' The requirement of perfection is repeated for each animal category. The repetition is pedagogical — Israel is trained through constant repetition to understand that no imperfect substitute can stand. Christ's sinlessness is not incidental but structurally required by the entire sacrificial system: 'a lamb without blemish or spot, foreknown before the foundation of the world' (1 Pet 1:19–20).
1.11: A shadow: 'And he shall kill it on the north side of the altar before YHWH.' The north side of the altar became the traditional slaughter site — the geography of sacrifice is defined. At Golgotha, the site of Christ's death was outside the north gate of Jerusalem, outside the camp (Heb 13:12) — matching the pattern that sin-offerings were burned outside the camp (Lev 4:12, 21).
1.12: A shadow: 'And he shall cut it into pieces, with its head and its fat, and the priest shall arrange them on the wood that is on the fire on the altar.' The care taken in the arrangement of every piece emphasizes completeness — no part left off. Christ's atonement is complete: 'It is finished' (John 19:30) — the Greek tetelestai is an accounting term for 'paid in full.'
1.13: A direct type: 'But the entrails and the legs he shall wash with water. And the priest shall offer all of it and burn it on the altar; it is a burnt offering, a food offering with a pleasing aroma to YHWH.' The repeated reah nichoach formula (vv. 9, 13, 17) hammers the note of divine acceptance. The cross was not defeat but acceptance — the Father accepted the sacrifice: 'Therefore God has highly exalted him' (Phil 2:9). Paul explicitly applies the Levitical formula: Christ 'gave himself up for us, a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God' (Eph 5:2).
1.14: A direct type: 'If his offering to YHWH is a burnt offering of birds, he shall bring his offering of turtledoves or pigeons.' The bird offering was the poor man's provision — the same offering Mary brought at Jesus's presentation (Luke 2:24: 'a pair of turtledoves or two young pigeons'), identifying Christ with the poor. James 2:5: 'Has not God chosen those who are poor in the world to be rich in faith?' Christ entered the sacrificial system from the lowest economic level of offering, signaling that atonement is accessible regardless of wealth.
1.15: A shadow: 'And the priest shall bring it to the altar and wring off its head and burn it on the altar. Its blood shall be drained out on the side of the altar.' The bird offering involves the priest's direct action — he himself wrings off the head. Christ's sacrifice involved direct divine action: 'It was the will of YHWH to crush him' (Isa 53:10). The Spirit came upon Jesus as a dove at his baptism (Matt 3:16) — marking him as the dove-offering that the Father himself would offer.
1.16: A shadow: 'He shall remove its crop with its contents and cast it beside the altar on the east side, in the place for ashes.' The crop is removed — what the bird has ingested, what it has fed on from the earth. Christ, though taking on human flesh, was without sin — 'holy, innocent, unstained, separated from sinners' (Heb 7:26). The removal of the crop is a type of Christ's complete freedom from what corrupts.
1.17: A direct type: 'He shall tear it open by its wings, but shall not sever it completely. And the priest shall burn it on the altar, on the wood that is on the fire. It is a burnt offering, a food offering with a pleasing aroma to YHWH.' The third repetition of reah nichoach closes chapter 1. The threefold repetition — for cattle (v9), small cattle (v13), birds (v17) — spans all economic levels of offering. At every level, the same result: a pleasing aroma accepted by God. The richest and the poorest brought the same acceptance. Christ, offered once, was accepted for all (Heb 10:14: 'by a single offering he has perfected for all time those who are being sanctified').

2.1: A direct type: 'When anyone brings a grain offering [minchah] as an offering to YHWH, his offering shall be of fine flour. He shall pour oil on it and put frankincense on it.' The grain offering's three elements are a three-way type: fine flour (solet) = Christ's sinless humanity — the finest grain, sifted without blemish; oil = the Holy Spirit — Jesus was anointed with the Spirit (Acts 10:38; Luke 4:18); frankincense = prayer and intercession — 'The prayers of the saints... golden bowls full of incense' (Rev 5:8). The grain offering is the type of the Incarnation.
2.2: A shadow: 'And he shall bring it to Aaron's sons the priests. And he shall take from it a handful of the fine flour and oil, with all of its frankincense, and the priest shall burn this as its memorial portion on the altar, a food offering with a pleasing aroma to YHWH.' The azkarah (memorial/reminder portion) represents the whole — a handful stands for the entire offering. Christ's one sacrifice represents and effects the whole of atonement: 'He has appeared once for all at the end of the ages to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself' (Heb 9:26).
2.3: A direct type: 'But the rest of the grain offering shall be for Aaron and his sons; it is a most holy part of YHWH's food offerings.' The priests eat the remainder of the grain offering — they are sustained by the sacrifice. Christ is both the offering and the bread: 'I am the bread of life' (John 6:35); 'the bread that I will give for the life of the world is my flesh' (John 6:51). The priests eating the grain offering type the believer's feeding on Christ in faith and in the Eucharist (1 Cor 10:16).
2.4: A direct type: 'When you bring a grain offering baked in the oven as an offering, it shall be unleavened loaves of fine flour mixed with oil, or unleavened wafers smeared with oil.' Unleavened (matzot) = without leaven = without sin. Paul interprets leaven as sin and malice (1 Cor 5:7–8): 'Cleanse out the old leaven... as you really are unleavened. For Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed.' The absence of leaven in the grain offering is the type of Christ's sinlessness — the fine flour of his humanity, tested and baked by suffering (Heb 2:10), contains no corruption.
2.5: A shadow: 'And if your offering is a grain offering cooked on a griddle, it shall be of fine flour unleavened, mixed with oil.' The multiple preparation forms (oven, griddle, pan) show that the sacrificial principle applies across varied forms — as does Christ's one sacrifice: applicable across all times, peoples, and circumstances.
2.6: A direct type: 'You shall break it in pieces and pour oil on it; it is a grain offering.' Breaking in pieces with oil poured over — a eucharistic image: 'He broke it and gave it to the disciples, saying, Take, eat; this is my body' (Matt 26:26). The grain offering of broken pieces with oil is the OT form of what Christ instituted at the Last Supper.
2.7: A shadow: 'And if your offering is a grain offering cooked in a pan, it shall be made of fine flour with oil.' The third preparation method; the consistent requirements — fine flour, oil, no leaven — remain across all forms. Christ's nature is constant across all expressions of his work: the same sinless humanity, the same Spirit-anointing, whether as teacher, healer, or sacrifice.
2.8: A shadow: 'And you shall bring the grain offering that is made of these things to YHWH, and when it is presented to the priest, he shall bring it to the altar.' The offering must be brought to the priest, who brings it to the altar. Christ is both the offerer who brings himself and the priest who presents the sacrifice — the author and perfecter of our faith who 'offered himself without blemish to God' (Heb 9:14).
2.9: A shadow: 'And the priest shall take from the grain offering its memorial portion and burn this on the altar, a food offering with a pleasing aroma to YHWH.' The azkarah burned on the altar is the portion that ascends — the part given wholly to God. In Christ's sacrifice, the offering is entirely God's: 'He offered himself without blemish to God' (Heb 9:14). The Father accepted it: 'a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God' (Eph 5:2).
2.10: A shadow: 'But the rest of the grain offering shall be for Aaron and his sons; it is a most holy part of YHWH's food offerings.' What is not burned belongs to the priests — holy food for holy service. The gospel makes believers a 'royal priesthood' (1 Pet 2:9) who feed on Christ. What Christ offers entirely to God, he then gives as food to his priests.
2.11: A direct type: 'No grain offering that you bring to YHWH shall be made with leaven, for you shall burn no leaven and no honey as a food offering to YHWH.' The absolute prohibition on leaven makes it the most explicit type in the grain offering: leaven = sin and corruption. 'Do you not know that a little leaven leavens the whole lump? Cleanse out the old leaven... for Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed' (1 Cor 5:6–7). The no-leaven rule announces that atonement requires a sinless sacrifice — only Christ qualifies.
2.12: A shadow: 'As an offering of firstfruits you may bring them to YHWH, but they shall not be offered on the altar for a pleasing aroma.' The graduated system: first-fruits may include leaven and honey but they cannot ascend as atoning sacrifice. Only what is without leaven can atone. This shows that even good things — the natural produce of the earth — cannot serve as an atoning sacrifice. Only the one without leaven can bear sin.
2.13: A direct type: 'You shall season all your grain offerings with salt. You shall not let the salt of the covenant with your God be lacking from your grain offering; with all your offerings you shall offer salt.' Salt = covenant permanence and incorruption (salt preserves). The 'covenant of salt' (berit melach) is the most permanent of covenants — it cannot be corrupted. Christ's sacrifice establishes 'the eternal covenant' (Heb 13:20) by his blood — the incorruptible salt-covenant that never expires.
2.14: A direct type: 'If you offer a grain offering of firstfruits to YHWH, you shall offer for the grain offering of your firstfruits fresh ears, roasted with fire, crushed new grain.' The first-fruits offering is a type of the resurrection. Paul: 'Christ has been raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep' (1 Cor 15:20). Jesus is the first-fruits grain — roasted and crushed (death and burial), but the first to rise from the ground in resurrection.
2.15: A shadow: 'And you shall put oil on it and lay frankincense on it; it is a grain offering.' Oil and frankincense on the first-fruits: the Spirit and prayer/intercession attend the first-fruits offering. At the resurrection, the Spirit who raised Jesus (Rom 8:11) is the same who anointed him. The risen Christ intercedes (Heb 7:25) — frankincense rising before God.
2.16: A shadow: 'And the priest shall burn the memorial portion, some of the crushed grain and some of the oil with all of its frankincense; it is a food offering to YHWH.' The first-fruits' azkarah burns as a pleasing aroma — the culminating promise of the grain offering chapter: even the first-fruits, roasted and crushed, are accepted by God. The resurrection is not a divine afterthought but an accepted offering: 'God... gave him glory, so that your faith and hope are in God' (1 Pet 1:21).

3.1: A direct type: 'If his offering is a sacrifice of peace offering [zebach shelamim], if he offers an animal from the herd, male or female, he shall offer it without blemish [tamim] before YHWH.' The shelamim (peace offering, from shalom) uniquely features the offerer eating from the sacrifice in communion with God and the priests. Christ is our peace: 'He himself is our peace, who has made us both one and has broken down in his flesh the dividing wall of hostility' (Eph 2:14). The communion meal of the peace offering is the type of the Lord's Supper — restored fellowship between God and man at the table.
3.2: A direct type: 'And he shall lay his hand on the head of his offering and kill it at the entrance of the tent of meeting, and Aaron's sons the priests shall throw the blood against the sides of the altar.' Hand-laying (semichah) appears again — the peace offering also requires identification, not just gratitude. The worshiper approaches not on the basis of his own worthiness but through substitutionary identification. Christ is the one who makes sinners acceptable to draw near (Heb 10:22: 'let us draw near with a true heart').
3.3: A direct type: 'And from the sacrifice of the peace offering, as a food offering to YHWH, he shall offer the fat covering the entrails and all the fat that is on the entrails.' The fat portions (the richest, most energetic parts of the animal) are given entirely to YHWH — not eaten, not given to priests. The richest belongs to God alone. Christ gave what was most precious: 'Though he was in the form of God, he did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped, but emptied himself' (Phil 2:6–7) — the ultimate giving of the richest for God's purpose.
3.4: A shadow: 'And the two kidneys with the fat that is on them at the loins, and the long lobe of the liver that he shall remove with the kidneys.' The internal anatomy given to God — what is hidden, internal, private. Christ's self-giving was internal and total: 'not my will, but yours, be done' (Luke 22:42). The kidneys in Hebrew thought (kelayot) were the seat of the inner life; giving them to God types the complete inner surrender of Gethsemane.
3.5: A shadow: 'Then Aaron's sons shall burn it on the altar on top of the burnt offering, which is on the wood on the fire; it is a food offering with a pleasing aroma to YHWH.' The peace offering burns on top of the burnt offering — it presupposes the olah beneath it. Peace with God (shelamim) rests on the foundation of total consecration (olah). Christ first consecrated himself entirely (John 17:19: 'I sanctify myself') and then made peace through the blood of his cross (Col 1:20).
3.6: A direct type: 'If his offering for a peace offering to YHWH is an animal from the flock, male or female, he shall offer it without blemish.' The peace offering uniquely accepts male or female — in this offering, the gender distinction of the olah (male only) is removed. This anticipates Paul's declaration: 'There is neither male nor female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus' (Gal 3:28). The peace Christ makes is equally available to all.
3.7: A direct type: 'If he offers a lamb for his offering, then he shall offer it before YHWH, lay his hand on the head of his offering, and kill it in front of the tent of meeting.' The lamb peace offering sets up the central NT image: Christ as 'the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world' (John 1:29). The hand-laying at the peace offering means the worshiper's need for peace — his estrangement from God — is transferred to the lamb.
3.8: A direct type: 'Aaron's sons shall throw its blood against the sides of the altar.' Blood-throwing at the altar marks the peace offering as still fundamentally an atonement rite — fellowship with God is possible only through blood-mediated reconciliation. Colossians 1:20: God was pleased 'through him to reconcile to himself all things, whether on earth or in heaven, making peace by the blood of his cross.'
3.9: A shadow: 'Then from the sacrifice of the peace offering he shall offer as a food offering to YHWH its fat; he shall remove the whole fat tail, cut off close to the backbone, and the fat that covers the entrails and all the fat that is on the entrails.' The fat tail (ha-alyah) — unique to broad-tailed sheep — is given entirely to YHWH. What is characteristically unique to the animal, its distinguishing feature, is offered to God. Christ offered what was uniquely his — his divine-human nature, the one thing no one else could give.
3.10: A shadow: 'And the two kidneys with the fat that is on them at the loins, and the long lobe of the liver that he shall remove with the kidneys.' Repeated from v4 for the sheep variety — the internal organs that represent the hidden life. Christ's offering was not merely external conduct but the hidden obedience of his inner life: 'he learned obedience through what he suffered' (Heb 5:8).
3.11: A direct type: 'And the priest shall burn it on the altar as a food offering to YHWH.' The peace offering is lechem isheh — 'food of the fire offering' or 'food offering.' God receives the sacrifice as nourishment — a figure of speech for acceptable worship. Christ calls himself the bread of life (John 6:35) and at the Eucharist, his body and blood are given as food: 'unless you eat the flesh of the Son of Man and drink his blood, you have no life in you' (John 6:53). The communion meal of the peace offering becomes the Lord's Supper.
3.12: A shadow: 'If his offering is a goat, then he shall offer it before YHWH.' The third animal option — goat. The broad inclusivity of the peace offering (cattle, sheep, goat) mirrors the universality of the peace Christ makes: 'he is the propitiation for our sins, and not for ours only but also for the sins of the whole world' (1 John 2:2).
3.13: A direct type: 'And he shall lay his hand on its head and kill it in front of the tent of meeting, and the sons of Aaron shall throw its blood against the sides of the altar.' The third repetition of hand-laying in chapter 3 (for cattle v2, sheep v7, goat v13). The triple repetition across all three animal types establishes identification/substitution as an absolute requirement — no peace offering without hand-laying, no peace with God without Christ bearing what we are.
3.14: A shadow: 'Then he shall offer from it, as his offering for a food offering to YHWH, the fat covering the entrails and all the fat that is on the entrails.' Fat portions again — the richest given to God. The goat's fat portions are identical to the cattle's and sheep's: the same principle governs all. The offering of the best to God is universal.
3.15: A shadow: 'And the two kidneys with the fat that is on them at the loins, and the long lobe of the liver that he shall remove with the kidneys.' The third occurrence of the kidney formula (vv4, 10, 15) — one for each animal type. The inner organs given to God type the complete inner surrender Christ made. Hebrews 10:7 applies Ps 40 to Christ: 'I have come to do your will, O God' — not outer compliance but the deepest inner obedience.
3.16: A direct type: 'And the priest shall burn them on the altar as a food offering with a pleasing aroma. All fat is YHWH's.' Kol-chelev laYHWH — 'all fat is YHWH's.' This is the absolute principle underlying the peace offering: the best, the richest, belongs to God alone. Christ's sacrifice was the offering of what was most precious to God — his own Son (Rom 8:32: 'He who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all'). The 'all fat is YHWH's' rule means nothing was withheld: 'he loved me and gave himself for me' (Gal 2:20).
3.17: A direct type: 'It shall be a statute forever throughout your generations, in all your dwelling places, that you eat neither fat nor blood.' The double prohibition — no fat, no blood — is the peace offering's lasting theological boundary. Blood cannot be consumed because blood = life, and life belongs to God; it must be given to God (v16's kol-chelev laYHWH). Christ's blood cannot simply be possessed or managed by humanity — it must be received as the gift of the one who owned it: 'This is my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins' (Matt 26:28). The no-blood-consumption rule prevents Israel from thinking of sacrifice as magic they control; it must be received as grace.

4.1: A shadow: YHWH spoke to Moses about the sin offering (chatat). The sin offering chapter introduces a new category: the offering for shegagah (unintentional sin, mistakes, errors). The Levitical system distinguishes sins done in error from those done 'with a high hand' (defiantly, Num 15:30–31). The chatat addresses the former. Christ's intercession from the cross covers both: 'Father, forgive them, for they know not what they do' (Luke 23:34); his atoning work covers the full range of human failure.
4.2: A direct type: 'Speak to the people of Israel, saying, If anyone sins unintentionally [bishgagah] in any of YHWH's commandments about things not to be done, and does any one of them...' The sin offering is specifically for unintentional violations — sins of ignorance, weakness, and error. Peter frames the crucifixion itself as an act done in ignorance: 'you acted in ignorance, as did also your rulers' (Acts 3:17). Christ's prayer from the cross applies the chatat logic directly: the unknowing transgressor is covered by the sacrificial provision.
4.3: A direct type: 'If it is the anointed priest who sins, thus bringing guilt on the people, then he shall offer for the sin that he has committed a bull from the herd without blemish to YHWH for a sin offering.' The anointed priest's sin offering is the most costly — a whole bull. The irony is profound: the one appointed to bring others near to God must first bring his own sin offering. Hebrews presses this contrast: 'For it was indeed fitting that we should have such a high priest, holy, innocent, unstained, separated from sinners... He has no need, like those high priests, to offer sacrifices daily, first for his own sins and then for those of the people' (Heb 7:26–27). The Levitical priest's self-atonement requirement is the very deficiency Christ removes.
4.4: A direct type: 'He shall bring the bull to the entrance of the tent of meeting before YHWH and lay his hand on the head of the bull and kill the bull before YHWH.' Hand-laying appears even in the priest's own sin offering — the priest identifies with the bull just as any layman would. The sacrificial system makes no exemption for the one performing it; everyone who approaches must come through identification with the substitute. Christ 'learned obedience through what he suffered' (Heb 5:8) — he approached through suffering, not exemption from it.
4.5: A direct type: 'And the anointed priest shall take some of the blood of the bull and bring it into the tent of meeting.' The sin offering's blood is brought inside the tent — unlike the burnt and peace offerings whose blood is thrown against the outer altar. The penetration of blood into the sanctuary is the key typological act. Hebrews 9:11–12: 'But when Christ appeared as a high priest of the good things that have come... he entered once for all into the holy places, not by means of the blood of goats and calves but by means of his own blood, thus securing an eternal redemption.'
4.6: A direct type: 'And the priest shall dip his finger in the blood and sprinkle part of the blood seven times before YHWH in front of the veil of the sanctuary.' Seven sprinklings before the veil — the veil that separated the holy place from the most holy. Seven = completeness; the blood of the sin offering is completely applied to the boundary between holy and most holy. At Christ's death, 'the curtain of the temple was torn in two, from top to bottom' (Matt 27:51) — the veil is not sprinkled but abolished. What the chatat blood reached toward, Christ's blood passed through entirely.
4.7: A shadow: 'And the priest shall put some of the blood on the horns of the altar of fragrant incense before YHWH that is in the tent of meeting, and all the rest of the blood of the bull he shall pour out at the base of the altar of burnt offering that is at the entrance of the tent of meeting.' Blood applied to both altars — the incense altar inside the tent and the burnt-offering altar outside. Christ's atonement reaches both: the inner sanctuary of prayer and the outer altar of sacrifice are both covered by his blood.
4.8: A shadow: 'And all the fat of the bull of the sin offering he shall remove from it, the fat covering the entrails and all the fat that is on the entrails.' The fat portions of the sin offering follow the same rule as the peace offering — given to YHWH. Even in the sin offering, the best is given to God: the sin-covering sacrifice does not merely manage sin but offers God what is richest.
4.9: A shadow: 'And the two kidneys with the fat that is on them at the loins, and the long lobe of the liver that he shall remove with the kidneys.' The inner organs — the seat of hidden life — given to God even in the sin offering. Christ's inner surrender to the Father in Gethsemane ('not my will, but yours, be done,' Luke 22:42) is the ultimate inner-organ offering: the will, the deepest kernel of the self, placed on the altar.
4.10: A shadow: 'Just as these are taken from the ox of the sacrifice of the peace offerings; and the priest shall burn them on the altar of burnt offering.'
