The 50th-year/50th-day pattern is not coincidental: both Pentecost and Jubilee are eschatological release celebrations. The Spirit poured out at Pentecost is the down payment of the eschatological Jubilee Christ inaugurates.
25.9: Type: The Jubilee is proclaimed by a ram’s horn (shofar teru'ah) on the Day of Atonement. The shofar blast is the sound of divine proclamation and eschatological announcement. 1 Thessalonians 4:16: at Christ’s return “the Lord himself will descend from heaven with a cry of command, with the voice of an archangel, and with the sound of the trumpet of God.” 1 Corinthians 15:52: “the trumpet will sound, and the dead will be raised imperishable.” The Jubilee shofar on Atonement Day foreshadows the trumpet that announces the final liberation at Christ’s return.
25.10: Direct type: “Proclaim liberty (deror) throughout the land to all its inhabitants.” This is the most direct Jubilee-Christ connection in the NT. Luke 4:16–21: Jesus reads Isaiah 61:1–2 in the Nazareth synagogue (“to proclaim liberty to the captives” — the same deror-vocabulary) and declares, “Today this Scripture has been fulfilled in your hearing.” Jesus claims to be the personal inauguration of the Jubilee — the year of the Lord’s favor made flesh.
25.11: Direct type: The Jubilee year is itself a sabbatical year — no sowing, no harvest of aftergrowth, no grape-gathering. The Jubilee intensifies and embodies all the sabbath principles. Christ’s declaration of “the year of the Lord’s favor” (Luke 4:19) frames his entire ministry as the Jubilee year: healings restore what was lost, sins are forgiven (debts canceled), the poor receive good news. His ministry is the enacted Jubilee.
25.12: Type: The Jubilee is “holy” (qodesh): its produce is eaten directly, not harvested commercially. Holiness removes from ordinary commercial exchange. 1 Corinthians 6:20: “you were bought with a price. So glorify God in your body.” The Christian’s status before God is the opposite of commercial: not slaves sold in the market but people redeemed at infinite cost who now belong wholly to God, outside the economy of human exchange.
25.13: Type: In the Jubilee, each person returns to their original property (ahuzzah). The restoration to ancestral inheritance is a central Jubilee action. 1 Peter 1:3–4: “we have been born again to a living hope through the resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead, to an inheritance that is imperishable, undefiled, and unfading, kept in heaven for you.” The return to ancestral land in Jubilee is a shadow of the eschatological inheritance restored to God’s people through Christ’s resurrection.
25.14: Theme: “You shall not wrong one another.” The ethical command embedded in the economic law grounds the Jubilee’s justice provisions in the relationship between neighbors. Leviticus 19:18 (love your neighbor as yourself) is the summary principle. Jesus cites this as the second great commandment (Matt 22:39). The anti-exploitation laws of the Jubilee are applications of the love-neighbor principle — Christ names the center what Leviticus builds toward.
25.15: Shadow: The pricing formula (sell by years remaining, not land itself) treats time as the commodity, not land. This reflects a theology of stewardship over ownership. Luke 16:12: “if you have not been faithful in that which is another’s, who will give you that which is your own?” The Jubilee pricing model treats all land as ultimately YHWH’s (v.23), making every transaction a stewardship of someone else’s property — the posture Christ calls his disciples to toward all possessions.
25.16: Shadow: Many years remaining = higher price; few years remaining = lower price. The inverse relationship of time-remaining to price models the transience of earthly possession. Matthew 6:19–20: “Do not lay up for yourselves treasures on earth, where moth and rust destroy... but lay up for yourselves treasures in heaven.” Earthly property is time-limited; heavenly treasure is not subject to the Jubilee reduction.
25.17: Revelation of God: “You shall not wrong one another, but you shall fear your God, for I am YHWH your God.” The divine name as the ground of economic ethics appears here as in the Decalogue: YHWH’s identity creates the moral obligation. Colossians 3:23–24: “Whatever you do, work heartily, as for the Lord and not for men, knowing that from the Lord you will receive the inheritance as your reward.” The fear of the Lord that anchors Levitical economic law is the same motivation Paul gives for Christian economic conduct.
25.18: Type: Obedience to YHWH’s statutes brings security in the land. The covenant-obedience-security sequence is consistently developed in Leviticus 26 and Deuteronomy. Christ is the one who perfectly fulfills this covenant: Matthew 3:17: “This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased.” His perfect obedience secures the inheritance for all who are united to him — the security the land-sabbath promised is now secured in the one who obeyed completely.
25.19: Type: “The land will yield its fruit, and you will eat your fill and dwell in it securely.” The promised fertility and security of the land anticipates the eschatological abundance of the new creation. Revelation 22:2: the tree of life bears fruit every month and its leaves heal the nations — a creation restored beyond the Edenic original. The Levitical promise of land-fertility and safety is a thumbnail of the new Jerusalem’s inexhaustible provision.
25.20: Theme: The anticipated objection — “What shall we eat in the seventh year?” — is a question of faith in YHWH’s provision. The Jubilee requires trust that goes beyond visible means. Matthew 6:25–26: “Do not be anxious about your life, what you will eat or what you will drink... Look at the birds of the air: they neither sow nor reap nor gather into barns, and yet your heavenly Father feeds them.” The sabbath-year question is the original form of the anxiety Jesus addresses.
25.21: Type: YHWH promises to command his blessing on the sixth year for a triple-yield harvest. The miraculous provision before the rest mirrors the manna-double-portion on the sixth day (Ex 16:22-26). 2 Corinthians 9:8: “God is able to make all grace abound to you, so that having all sufficiency in all things at all times, you may abound in every good work.” The principle of divine provision exceeding normal limits is the Jubilee logic applied to grace.
25.22: Shadow: Eating the old crop into the eighth year while the new crop is growing models patience and trust in overlapping provisions. Philippians 4:19: “my God will supply every need of yours according to his riches in glory in Christ Jesus.” The promise that last year’s provision stretches to cover the gap before new growth arrives models the sufficiency of Christ’s resources for every season of the believer’s life.
25.23: Direct type: “The land shall not be sold in perpetuity, for the land is mine (ki li ha-aretz). For you are strangers and sojourners with me.” YHWH’s ownership of everything is the theological ground of the Jubilee. 1 Corinthians 6:19–20: “you are not your own, for you were bought with a price.” The Christian’s status is the NT equivalent of the Israelite’s: not owners but stewards of what belongs to God, and therefore free from the anxiety of maintaining what was never theirs to keep.
25.24: Type: The right of redemption (ge'ullah) throughout the land guarantees that alienated property can always return. The go'el system makes redemption structurally available. Christ is the ultimate kinsman-redeemer. 1 Peter 1:18–19: “you were ransomed from the futile ways inherited from your forefathers, not with perishable things such as silver or gold, but with the precious blood of Christ, like that of a lamb without blemish or spot.” The go'el’s right to pay redemption is fulfilled in Christ who pays what we cannot pay.
25.25: Type: “His nearest redeemer (go'alo ha-qarov) shall come and redeem what his brother has sold.” The kinsman-redeemer must be qarov — near, close. Christ as redeemer takes on human nature to become near. Hebrews 2:14–17: “Since therefore the children share in flesh and blood, he himself likewise partook of the same things... Therefore he had to be made like his brothers in every respect, so that he might become a merciful and faithful high priest.” Incarnation is the act of becoming the nearest kinsman.
25.26: Type: Self-redemption when resources allow is also valid. Christ as the one who had infinite resources chose to use them for redemption. John 10:17–18: “No one takes my life from me, but I lay it down of my own accord. I have authority to lay it down, and I have authority to take it up again.” His redemption is not compelled by circumstance but chosen from abundance — not a desperate kinsman with just enough, but one with all authority who freely pays the redemption price.
25.27: Shadow: The refund calculation (years remaining × annual yield) when self-redeeming shows that redemption is a precise accounting, not a rough settlement. Romans 5:8: “while we were still sinners, Christ died for us.” The atonement is not an approximate or partial payment but a precise and complete one: “it is finished” (John 19:30). The Jubilee redemption arithmetic points to the completeness of Christ’s atoning work.
25.28: Type: If redemption is not possible, the land returns automatically at Jubilee — the absolute backstop. No family can be permanently dispossessed. Romans 8:38–39: “neither death nor life, nor angels nor rulers, nor things present nor things to come, nor powers, nor height nor depth, nor anything else in all creation, will be able to separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord.” The Jubilee’s guaranteed return is a shadow of the security of the believer’s inheritance in Christ.
25.29: Shadow: The one-year redemption window for walled-city houses acknowledges that urban property operates differently from tribal land. The flexibility of the law to distinguish context mirrors the gospel’s adaptation to different human situations. 1 Corinthians 9:19–22: Paul becomes “all things to all people” — the same gospel applied with contextual sensitivity to different circumstances, as the Jubilee law applies its principles with contextual sensitivity to different property types.
25.30: Shadow: Urban houses in walled cities that are not redeemed within the year become permanent property of the buyer — the Jubilee does not apply. The limit of the Jubilee’s reach acknowledges the complexity of urban economic life. Luke 18:29–30: those who leave houses for the kingdom receive “many times more in this time, and in the age to come eternal life.” The things the Jubilee cannot restore in this age, the age to come restores abundantly.
25.31: Shadow: Unwalled village houses return at Jubilee — treated as rural land. The distinction between permanent city and impermanent village mirrors the NT distinction between the present age and the age to come. Hebrews 13:14: “here we have no lasting city, but we seek the city that is to come.” The Christian posture toward earthly possessions is like the unwalled-village house: held loosely, knowing that Jubilee-restoration is coming.
25.32: Type: Levitical city houses are always redeemable because the Levites have no tribal land allotment. The Levites are protected from permanent dispossession by a special provision. Christ as the fulfillment of the Levitical priesthood is the special provision that ensures his priestly people are never permanently lost. John 10:28–29: “I give them eternal life, and they will never perish, and no one will snatch them out of my hand.”
25.33: Shadow: Levitical houses always return at Jubilee — nothing can permanently separate the Levites from their cities. The guaranteed return of the priestly tribe to their communities anticipates the guarantee of the priestly people’s return to their inheritance. 1 Peter 2:9: all believers are “a royal priesthood” whose inheritance is guaranteed by Christ. The special protection for Levites becomes, in Christ, the universal protection of all who serve God.
25.34: Revelation of God: Levitical pasture land is completely inalienable — it can never be sold. The absolute protection of what is needed for the priestly service reveals YHWH’s commitment to maintaining the infrastructure of worship. Hebrews 7:24: Christ “holds his priesthood permanently, because he continues forever.” What the inalienable Levitical pastureland protects in type, Christ’s eternal priesthood secures in reality.
25.35: Type: “If your brother becomes poor and cannot maintain himself with you, you shall support him... and he shall live with you.” The command to let the destitute brother “live with you” (vechai 'immakh) is the positive social corollary of the Jubilee. John 14:3: “I will come again and will take you to myself, that where I am you may be also.” The command to let the impoverished brother live alongside you foreshadows Christ’s bringing his people to dwell with him permanently.
25.36: Type: No interest (neshekh) on loans to a brother — the prohibition of exploitative gain from another’s desperation. 2 Corinthians 8:9: “though he was rich, yet for your sake he became poor, so that you by his poverty might become rich.” Christ’s incarnation is the anti-usury principle applied at cosmic scale: not taking advantage of human need but entering human poverty to enrich it. The interest-prohibition is a shadow of grace’s anti-exploitation logic.
25.37: Theme: No interest on money, no profit on food given to a brother in need. The two categories (money + food) cover all basic economic relationships. Acts 2:44–45: the early Jerusalem church practiced a voluntary version of this: “all who believed were together and had all things in common. And they were selling their possessions and belongings and distributing the proceeds to all, as any had need.” The Pentecost community enacted the Jubilee’s anti-exploitation economics as a fruit of the Spirit’s outpouring.
25.38: Type: “I am YHWH your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt to give you the land of Canaan, and to be your God.” The Exodus covenant-identity is the ground of economic ethics. Colossians 1:13–14: “He has delivered us from the domain of darkness and transferred us to the kingdom of his beloved Son, in whom we have redemption, the forgiveness of sins.” The new Exodus accomplished by Christ creates the same identity-based ethical obligation — the redeemed community lives by the economics of the Redeemer.
25.39: Type: An impoverished Israelite who sells himself cannot be treated as a slave but must be treated as a hired worker and sojourner. The refusal to commodify the Israelite person is grounded in covenant identity. Philemon 16: Paul asks Philemon to receive the formerly enslaved Onesimus “no longer as a bondservant but better than a bondservant, as a dear brother” — the gospel transforms the master-slave relationship by the same logic: the one bought with Christ’s blood cannot be treated as mere property.
25.40: Shadow: The debt-worker serves until Jubilee, then goes free with his children. Family restoration is part of the Jubilee release — not just personal freedom but communal reintegration. Ephesians 2:19: “you are no longer strangers and aliens, but you are fellow citizens with the saints and members of the household of God.” Christ’s Jubilee-liberation restores not only individuals but their belonging to community — the family goes free together.
25.41: Type: He returns to his family and to the possession of his fathers. The double restoration (family + land) is the Jubilee’s social vision of wholeness. Romans 8:23: we “wait eagerly for adoption as sons, the redemption of our bodies.” The bodily resurrection and the restoration of creation are the eschatological equivalents of the Jubilee’s physical return: full embodied restoration to the family of God and the inheritance of the new creation.
25.42: Direct type: “For they are my servants (avadai) whom I brought out of the land of Egypt; they shall not be sold as slaves.” YHWH’s ownership claim — they are already his servants because of the Exodus — is the ground of their freedom from other masters. 1 Corinthians 7:22–23: “he who was called in the Lord as a bondservant is a freedman of the Lord. Likewise he who was free when called is a bondservant of Christ. You were bought with a price; do not become bondservants of men.” The NT exactly reapplies the Jubilee logic to the believer’s new covenant identity.
25.43: Type: “You shall not rule over him ruthlessly (be-farekhk), but shall fear your God.” Parekhk is the crushing labor of Egypt (Ex 1:13-14). The prohibition is: do not be what Egypt was to Israel. Matthew 11:28–30: “Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest... my yoke is easy, and my burden is light.” Christ’s “easy yoke” is the explicit antithesis of the Egyptian parekhk — he has come to replace crushing labor with rest.
25.44: Shadow: Foreign slaves may be held permanently. This provision, jarring in context, reflects the limit of the covenant community’s reach in the old covenant era. The gospel dissolves this limit. Galatians 3:28: in Christ “there is neither Jew nor Greek, there is neither slave nor free.” Colossians 3:11: “here there is not Greek and Jew, circumcised and uncircumcised, barbarian, Scythian, slave, free; but Christ is all, and in all.” The ethnic and social distinctions that structured the old covenant’s limited reach are overcome in Christ.
25.45: Shadow: Resident aliens and their locally-born descendants could also be enslaved. This reflects the old covenant’s limited application of covenant protections to non-Israelites. Ephesians 2:11–13: Paul addresses those who were “strangers to the covenants of promise, having no hope and without God in the world” but who have now been “brought near by the blood of Christ.” The gospel extends the Jubilee’s protections to those who were once outside its scope.
25.46: Shadow: Foreign slaves could be bequeathed as permanent property. The institution persists within the old covenant economy. Philemon 15–16: Paul addresses exactly this situation — an enslaved person now in Christ — and says to receive him “as a dear brother... both in the flesh and in the Lord.” The gospel does not immediately overturn social structures but transforms the relationships within them from the inside, a different but more radical movement than the Jubilee’s external reset.
25.47: Type: An Israelite who becomes enslaved to a wealthy resident alien faces the most complex redemption case. The reversal of normal social hierarchy (Israelite serving a foreigner) mirrors the incarnation’s logic. Philippians 2:7–8: Christ “emptied himself, by taking the form of a servant... he humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death.” The go’el must reach into a foreign master’s household to redeem his kin; Christ enters the domain of sin and death to redeem his people.
25.48: Type: Even when sold to a foreigner, the kinsman-redeemer (go'el) has the right to redeem. Romans 8:31–32: “If God is for us, who can be against us? He who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all, how will he not also with him graciously give us all things?” The go'el’s right to redemption regardless of who holds his kinsman mirrors God’s unassailable right to redeem his people from any power that holds them.
25.49: Type: The hierarchy of redeemers (brother, uncle, cousin, clansman, self) creates concentric circles of responsibility. Hebrews 2:11–12: “For he who sanctifies and those who are sanctified all have one source. That is why he is not ashamed to call them brothers, saying, ‘I will tell of your name to my brothers.’” Christ as kinsman-redeemer establishes his kinship by sharing human nature — making himself the nearest possible go'el.
25.50: Shadow: The proportional redemption calculation (years remaining × annual value) treats the person’s labor as having a precise time-value. 1 Corinthians 6:20: “you were bought with a price.” The price paid for believers is not an approximate or partial redemption but a complete purchase. Christ’s blood is not a down payment on a long-term installment plan but a once-for-all price that fully satisfies the claim (Heb 9:12).
25.51: Shadow: If many years remain, a large buyback price is required. The greater the time of service remaining, the more costly the redemption. Romans 5:6–8: “while we were still weak... while we were still sinners, Christ died for us.” The costliness of redemption is not reduced by the condition of the one redeemed. Christ pays the full price regardless of how deep in slavery his people are when he reaches them.
25.52: Shadow: Few years remaining = smaller redemption price. Even a near-Jubilee redemption, though cheaper, is still a real payment. Luke 15:4–7: the shepherd leaves the ninety-nine to find the one lost sheep. No person is too small a redemption investment to be worth seeking. The Jubilee’s proportional pricing does not diminish the importance of any individual redemption.
25.53: Type: “He shall treat him as a worker hired year by year. He shall not rule over him ruthlessly (be-farekhk) in your sight.” Community accountability for how workers are treated mirrors the church’s corporate responsibility. Galatians 6:1–2: “Brothers, if anyone is caught in any transgression, you who are spiritual should restore him in a spirit of gentleness... Bear one another’s burdens, and so fulfill the law of Christ.” The community’s watchful care against “crushing labor” is fulfilled in the church’s burden-bearing.
25.54: Type: Even without a redeemer, the Jubilee provides automatic release. The guaranteed freedom regardless of external conditions is the strongest anti-slavery provision in the Levitical law. Romans 8:1: “There is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus.” The believer’s freedom in Christ is as automatic and unconditional as the Jubilee release: not dependent on human intermediary or favorable circumstance but secured by divine appointment.
25.55: Direct type: “For it is to me that the people of Israel are servants. They are my servants whom I brought out of the land of Egypt: I am YHWH your God.” The chapter closes where it began: YHWH’s ownership claim grounded in the Exodus. 1 Corinthians 6:19–20: “you are not your own, for you were bought with a price. So glorify God in your body.” The new covenant Exodus accomplished by Christ creates the same identity: not your own, belonging wholly to the one who redeemed you. The Jubilee’s final word is the gospel’s first word: you have been bought — therefore live as the free servants of the one who bought you.

26.1: An allusion: 'You shall not make idols for yourselves or erect an image or pillar.' The idol prohibition opens the covenant's blessing-and-curse section — the first commandment grounds the whole. Christ is the true image: 'He is the image (eikon) of the invisible God' (Col 1:15). The prohibition against crafted images is the negative side of which Christ's incarnation is the positive: the uncraftable, unimaginable God has given humanity his own true image in the Son.
26.2: An allusion: 'You shall keep my Sabbaths and reverence my sanctuary: I am YHWH.' Christ is Lord of the Sabbath (Mark 2:28; Matt 12:8) — not merely a keeper of the Sabbath command but its source and fulfillment. 'The Sabbath was made for man, not man for the Sabbath' — Christ reinterprets the Sabbath's purpose as his own to define. His resurrection on the first day reshapes the Sabbath into the Lord's Day, the new-creation Sabbath.
26.3: An allusion: 'If you walk in my statutes and observe my commandments and do them...' — the conditional clause that opens the blessing section. Christ is the only one who fully met this condition. His perfect obedience (Phil 2:8; Heb 5:8-9) is the ground on which the blessings of vv.4-13 flow to his people. The conditionality of Leviticus 26 is not abolished but fulfilled: Christ fulfills the condition so that those in him receive the blessings unconditionally.
26.4: An allusion: the rain in its season and the harvest blessing follow covenant faithfulness. Christ's perfect obedience to the Father brings the harvest of the redeemed: 'Unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit' (John 12:24). The agricultural harvest of Lev 26:4 is the natural type; the harvest of souls gathered by Christ's death and resurrection is the spiritual fulfillment.
26.5: An allusion: threshing reaching to the grape harvest, grape harvest reaching to the sowing — unending abundance following obedience. Amos 9:13 echoes this vision: 'The plowman shall overtake the reaper.' Christ's kingdom is described in harvest terms throughout the Gospels (Matt 9:37-38; 13:23; John 4:35-36). The abundance of Lev 26:5 is the type of the kingdom harvest that knows no end.
26.6: A direct type: 'I will give peace in the land... and the sword shall not go through your land.' Christ is 'the Prince of Peace' (Isa 9:6) who 'made peace by the blood of his cross' (Col 1:20). 'He himself is our peace, who has made us both one and has broken down in his flesh the dividing wall of hostility' (Eph 2:14). The peace of the blessed land in Lev 26:6 — security from enemies, harmony in the community — is the type of the comprehensive shalom Christ brings.
26.7: An allusion: five chasing a hundred, the miraculous multiplication of power in victory. Christ's disciples went out as 'sheep among wolves' (Matt 10:16) — the ratio was impossibly against them — yet the early church's expansion shows precisely this pattern: small numbers producing disproportionate impact under the Spirit's power (Acts 2:41; 4:4).
26.8: An allusion: 'Five of you shall chase a hundred, and a hundred of you shall chase ten thousand, and your enemies shall fall before you by the sword.' The disproportionate victory covenant. Christ promises the same to his church: 'I will build my church, and the gates of hell shall not prevail against it' (Matt 16:18). The gates of Hades — the defensive fortifications of death itself — cannot withstand the advance of Christ's people.
26.9: An allusion: 'I will turn to you and make you fruitful and multiply you and confirm my covenant with you.' The Abrahamic fruitfulness promise renewed through covenant obedience. Christ as the seed of Abraham through whom all nations are blessed (Gal 3:16); the church as the Abrahamic family multiplied beyond reckoning (Gal 3:29). The new covenant 'confirms' (establishes firmly, Heb 9:15-17) what the old covenant anticipated.
26.10: An allusion: 'You shall eat old store long kept, and you shall clear out the old to make way for the new.' The old harvest cleared to make room for the new. The new covenant making way by superseding — not destroying — the old: 'What is becoming obsolete and growing old is ready to vanish away' (Heb 8:13). Christ does not patch the old garment; he brings the new wine that requires new wineskins (Matt 9:16-17).
26.11: A direct type: 'I will make my dwelling (mishkan) among you, and my soul shall not abhor you.' The divine dwelling among Israel is the tabernacle promise. John 1:14 fulfills it: 'The Word became flesh and tabernacled (eskēnōsen) among us.' The same root (shakan/mishkan) underlies both the tabernacle and John's incarnation description. In Christ the mishkan promise becomes literal: God's presence is not in a tent but in a human body.
26.12: A direct fulfillment: 'And I will walk among you and will be your God, and you shall be my people.' The foundational covenant formula. Paul quotes this verse in 2 Corinthians 6:16 as the basis for the church's identity as the temple of the living God: 'For we are the temple of the living God; as God said: I will make my dwelling among them and walk among them.' The new covenant community is the fulfillment of the walking-among-you promise — YHWH tabernacling in his Spirit-indwelt people.
26.13: An allusion: 'I am YHWH your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt, that you should not be their slaves. And I have broken the bars of your yoke and made you walk erect.' Christ's easy yoke replaces the crushing yoke: 'Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest. Take my yoke upon you and learn from me, for I am gentle and lowly in heart, and you will find rest for your souls. For my yoke is easy, and my burden is light' (Matt 11:28-30). He broke the bars of the law's condemning power (Gal 3:13; Col 2:14) to make the redeemed walk erect in dignity.
26.14: An allusion: 'But if you will not listen to me and will not do all these commandments...' — the curse section begins. This is the legal framework within which Christ's atoning work operates: the curse is real; the covenant threatened real consequences. Galatians 3:10: 'All who rely on works of the law are under a curse, for it is written: Cursed is everyone who does not abide by all things written in the Book of the Law and do them.' The 'if' of Lev 26:14 becomes the 'since' of human reality; the 'but' of grace enters in Christ.
26.15: An allusion: rejecting YHWH's statutes and breaking the covenant — this is the condition that makes the curse operative. The ultimate rejection of the covenant was the rejection of Christ himself (John 1:11). Yet in a stunning reversal, the one rejected became the bearer of the rejection's consequences: 'He was despised and rejected by men' (Isa 53:3) precisely in order that those who rejected him might be forgiven.
26.16: An allusion: the first curse tier — diseases, wasting, fever, eyes consumed, life drained. Christ took on all the infirmities of the curse: 'He took our illnesses and bore our diseases' (Matt 8:17, quoting Isa 53:4). The healing ministry of Jesus was not incidental to his mission but its enacted sign: he absorbed the curse's symptoms in his own body and began reversing them in others, previewing the total reversal of the curse at the resurrection.
26.17: An allusion: 'I will set my face against you, and you shall be struck down before your enemies.' The curse of divine opposition — YHWH's face turned against rather than toward. Christ on the cross experienced the ultimate form of this: 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' (Matt 27:46). The God-abandonment of Psalm 22 is the total form of what Lev 26:17 threatens: the covenant God turning his face away.
26.18: An allusion: 'And if in spite of this you will not listen to me, then I will discipline you again sevenfold for your sins.' The sevenfold escalation of judgment. Christ bore not a minimal judgment but a complete one — the full weight of divine wrath against sin (Rom 3:25-26; 1 John 2:2). The 'sevenfold' completeness of the curse in Lev 26:18, 21, 24, 28 corresponds to the completeness of atonement Christ made: 'It is finished' (John 19:30).
26.19: An allusion: 'I will break the pride of your power, and I will make your heavens like iron and your earth like bronze.' The arrogance that produces covenant failure is crushed. Christ, by contrast, 'humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross' (Phil 2:8). His was the opposite of the pride that draws the curse — his self-emptying obedience is the ground of his people's exaltation (Phil 2:9-11).
26.20: An allusion: 'And your strength shall be spent in vain, for your land shall not yield its increase, and the trees of the land shall not yield their fruit.' The futility curse — labor that produces nothing. Paul describes the creation's futility in similar terms (Rom 8:20-21) and the new creation's liberation from it. Christ's resurrection is the firstfruits of the liberation from futility: 'Your labor in the Lord is not in vain' (1 Cor 15:58).
26.21: An allusion: the second curse tier — the sevenfold plague escalation for ongoing disobedience. The graduated nature of the curse reflects the graduated nature of divine patience: YHWH gives opportunity after opportunity to repent before the full curse falls. Christ's ministry echoed this: 'O Jerusalem, Jerusalem, the city that kills the prophets... How often would I have gathered your children together as a hen gathers her brood under her wings, and you were not willing!' (Matt 23:37).
26.22: An allusion: 'I will let loose the wild beasts against you, which shall bereave you of your children and destroy your livestock and make you few in number.' The created order turning against the covenant people. In contrast, when Christ faced his wilderness temptation he was 'with the wild animals, and the angels were ministering to him' (Mark 1:13) — a deliberate echo of Eden's lost harmony, restored in the new Adam. Where covenant failure unleashes wild beasts, Christ's presence restores the peaceable kingdom (Isa 11:6-9).
26.23: An allusion: 'And if by this discipline you are not turned to me but walk contrary to me...' — the refusal to repent even under judgment. The hardness of heart that refuses chastening was the spiritual condition of the generation that rejected Christ (John 12:40; Matt 13:15). The curse section of Lev 26 traces the progressive hardening that ultimately required not more chastening but a wholly new redemptive act.
26.24: An allusion: 'Then I also will walk contrary to you, and I myself will strike you sevenfold for your sins.' The divine-contrary posture mirrors the human-contrary posture — the relationship has broken down completely. Christ came precisely at the point of this total relational breakdown: 'But when the fullness of time had come, God sent forth his Son, born of woman, born under the law, to redeem those who were under the law' (Gal 4:4-5).
26.25: An allusion: 'I will bring a sword upon you, that shall execute vengeance for the covenant.' The sword of covenant enforcement — the judgment side of the legal covenant. Zechariah 13:7: 'Awake, O sword, against my shepherd, against the man who stands next to me.' Christ quoted this verse at Gethsemane (Matt 26:31): the covenant sword fell on the shepherd rather than the sheep. The covenant's punishing sword was borne by the mediator.
26.26: A direct type: 'When I break your supply of bread, ten women shall bake your bread in a single oven and shall dole out your bread again by weight, and you shall eat and not be satisfied.' The curse of insufficient bread is the negative image of Christ's miraculous feeding: 'I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger, and whoever believes in me shall never thirst' (John 6:35). Where the curse produces scarcity and dissatisfaction, Christ produces superabundance: 'They all ate and were satisfied, and they took up twelve baskets full of broken pieces' (Matt 14:20).
26.27: An allusion: continued disobedience despite the staff of bread being broken — still not turning. The stubbornness of the human heart under judgment illustrates why the incarnation was necessary: no amount of external pressure produces the internal transformation only Christ can accomplish. 'I will give you a new heart and a new spirit I will put within you. And I will remove the heart of stone from your flesh and give you a heart of flesh' (Ezek 36:26).
26.28: An allusion: 'I will walk contrary to you in fury, and I myself will discipline you sevenfold for your sins.' The divine fury — the wrath of the covenant God against covenant-breaking. This is the wrath that Christ absorbed: 'God put him forward as a propitiation by his blood' (Rom 3:25). The fury of Lev 26:28 is the reality that makes propitiation necessary; Christ's blood is the answer the curse section anticipates.
26.29: An allusion: the extremity of the curse — cannibalism of sons and daughters, the ultimate sign of covenant breakdown. This was literally fulfilled in the sieges of Jerusalem (2 Kgs 6:28-29; Lam 4:10) as Jesus foresaw the same for 70 CE (Luke 21:20-24). Christ wept over Jerusalem (Luke 19:41-44) knowing the curse's full weight would fall there. The depths of sin's consequences that Lev 26:29 describes are the depths into which Christ descended to redeem.
26.30: An allusion: 'I will destroy your high places and cut down your incense altars and cast your dead bodies upon the dead bodies of your idols, and my soul will abhor you.' The destruction of false worship. Christ triumphed over all false spiritual powers: 'He disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him' (Col 2:15). The cross dismantled the system of false mediation between humanity and God — the high places made redundant by the true high priest.
26.31: A direct type: 'And I will lay your cities waste and will make your sanctuaries desolate.' Christ predicted the literal fulfillment of this for the temple: 'There will not be left here one stone upon another that will not be thrown down' (Matt 24:2; fulfilled 70 CE). But he also declared himself the replacement sanctuary: 'Destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up... he was speaking about the temple of his body' (John 2:19-21). The desolated sanctuary is the shadow; Christ as the eternal temple is the fulfillment.
26.32: An allusion: 'And I myself will devastate the land, so that your enemies who settle in it shall be appalled by it.' The exile — Israel's land under desolation. The exile's desolation created the longing for the one who would restore: the prophetic hope generated in the exile (Isaiah, Jeremiah, Ezekiel) is the most Christologically rich period of the OT. The desolation of Lev 26:32 is what made Isa 40:1-2's 'Comfort, comfort my people' so urgent.
26.33: An allusion: 'And I will scatter you among the nations, and I will unsheathe the sword after you, and your land shall be a desolation, and your cities shall be a waste.' The Diaspora — Israel scattered. Paul interprets the Diaspora as part of YHWH's plan to bring the gospel to the nations: 'through their trespass salvation has come to the Gentiles' (Rom 11:11). The scattering becomes, paradoxically, the seed-spreading of YHWH's purposes.
26.34: An allusion: 'Then the land shall enjoy its Sabbaths as long as it lies desolate, while you are in your enemies' land; then the land shall rest, and enjoy its Sabbaths.' The enforced Sabbath rest during exile. Hebrews 4:1-11 develops the Sabbath-rest theology: 'There remains a Sabbath rest for the people of God' — the rest that was not fully achieved in the land-promise, the Canaan-rest pointing beyond itself to the rest in Christ. The land's exile-Sabbath anticipates the eternal rest.
26.35: An allusion: the land rests in exile, making up for the Sabbath years Israel did not observe. Christ's final rest — 'It is finished' (John 19:30) — is the ultimate Sabbath declaration: the work of redemption is complete, the debt is paid, the rest is secured. Hebrews 4:10: 'Whoever has entered God's rest has also rested from his works as God did from his.'
26.36: An allusion: 'As for those of you who are left, I will send faintness into their hearts in the lands of their enemies. The sound of a driven leaf shall put them to flight, and they shall flee as one flees from the sword.' The spiritual desolation of the scattered people — fear, disorientation, loss of identity. Christ came to gather the scattered: 'He gathers the outcasts of Israel' (Ps 147:2); 'I will seek the lost, and I will bring back the strayed' (Ezek 34:16). The Great Shepherd comes for the frightened, faint-hearted exiles.
26.37: An allusion: 'They shall stumble over one another, as if to escape a sword, though none pursues.' The disarray of the cursed community — stumbling without even a real threat. Christ is the one who gathers the stumbling into a community that walks together: 'I am the light of the world. Whoever follows me will not walk in darkness, but will have the light of life' (John 8:12). The stumbling in darkness of Lev 26:37 contrasts with the sure-footed walking in light that Christ provides.
26.38: An allusion: 'And you shall perish among the nations, and the land of your enemies shall eat you up.' The threat of permanent assimilation and loss. YHWH's preservation of a remnant through exile was against all odds — precisely what made the return and eventual Messiah-coming possible. Christ came as the fulfillment of the preserved remnant: 'I did not come to abolish the Law or the Prophets but to fulfill them' (Matt 5:17) — the remnant-bearer of all that the exile threatened to destroy.
26.39: An allusion: 'And those of you who are left shall rot away in your enemies' lands because of their iniquity, and also because of the iniquities of their fathers they shall rot away like them.' The inherited iniquity — the sins of fathers transmitted to children. Christ broke the cycle of inherited guilt: 'God made him who had no sin to be sin for us, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God' (2 Cor 5:21). The generational rot ends where the one without inherited iniquity absorbs what humanity accumulated.
26.40: An allusion: 'But if they confess their iniquity and the iniquity of their fathers in their treachery... then I will remember my covenant.' The turning point — confession as the gateway to covenant restoration. 'If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness' (1 John 1:9). The restoration formula of Lev 26:40 is the shadow of the new covenant's forgiveness, grounded not in Israel's confession per se but in Christ's atoning work that makes confession efficacious.
26.41: A direct type: 'And walk contrary to me, so that I walked contrary to them and brought them into the land of their enemies — if then their uncircumcised heart is humbled...' The uncircumcised heart is the problem requiring not just external reform but internal transformation. Paul: 'No one is a Jew who is merely one outwardly, nor is circumcision outward and physical. But a Jew is one inwardly, and circumcision is a matter of the heart, by the Spirit, not by the letter' (Rom 2:28-29). Christ gives the circumcision of the heart (Col 2:11) that the covenant always required but could not itself produce.
26.42: A direct fulfillment: 'Then I will remember my covenant with Jacob, and I will remember my covenant with Isaac and my covenant with Abraham, and I will remember the land.' The Abrahamic covenant as the bedrock beneath the Sinai structure. Galatians 3:16: 'Now the promises were made to Abraham and to his offspring. It does not say, And to offsprings, referring to many, but to one, And to your offspring, who is Christ.' Christ is the specific seed through whom all three patriarchal covenant strands — Abraham, Isaac, Jacob — are fulfilled.
26.43: An allusion: the land enjoys its Sabbath in desolation because Israel did not observe the Sabbath years. Even in the judgment, the creation structure persists — YHWH's order cannot finally be violated without consequence. The Jubilee and Sabbath-year system that created this dynamic is what Paul has in mind when he speaks of the law as a custodian/guardian (Gal 3:24-25): the covenant's legal structures held things in place until the fullness of time when Christ came to fulfill them.
26.44: A direct fulfillment: 'Yet for all that, when they are in the land of their enemies, I will not spurn them, neither will I abhor them so as to destroy them utterly and break my covenant with them, for I am YHWH their God.' The unconditional preservation promise embedded within the curses. Paul quotes the prophetic expression of this in Romans 11:26-29: 'All Israel will be saved... As regards election, they are beloved for the sake of their forefathers. For the gifts and calling of God are irrevocable.' YHWH's 'not destroy them utterly' of Lev 26:44 is the root of Paul's 'gifts and calling are irrevocable.'
26.45: A direct fulfillment: 'But I will for their sake remember the covenant with their forefathers, whom I brought out of the land of Egypt in the sight of the nations, that I might be their God: I am YHWH.' Hebrews 8:6: Christ 'is the mediator of a better covenant, which is enacted on better promises.' The covenant YHWH promised to remember in Lev 26:45 is ultimately the new covenant mediated by Christ — built on the better promises of Jeremiah 31:31-34, grounded in the better sacrifice of the Son. The 'I will remember' of Lev 26:45 is confirmed in Christ's blood: 'This cup is the new covenant in my blood' (1 Cor 11:25).
26.46: A direct type: 'These are the statutes and rules and laws that YHWH made between himself and the people of Israel through Moses on Mount Sinai.' The closing formula of the Holiness Code and the body of Leviticus. Christ: 'Do not think that I have come to abolish the Law or the Prophets; I have not come to abolish them but to fulfill them' (Matt 5:17). Every statute of the Holiness Code — the sacrifice regulations, the purity laws, the festival calendar, the blessings and curses — is fulfilled in Christ. He is the end (telos, goal/completion) of the law (Rom 10:4).

27.1: An allusion: 'YHWH spoke to Moses, saying...' — the final section of Leviticus opens with the same divine speech formula as every other section. Christ is the greater mediator: 'Long ago, at many times and in many ways, God spoke to our fathers by the prophets, but in these last days he has spoken to us by his Son' (Heb 1:1-2). The law given through Moses is the penultimate word; Christ is the ultimate and definitive Word of God.
27.2: An allusion: persons dedicated by a special vow to YHWH. The complete self-dedication of Jesus to the Father's will is the fulfillment of every human vow of dedication: 'My food is to do the will of him who sent me and to accomplish his work' (John 4:34). 'I have come down from heaven, not to do my own will but the will of him who sent me' (John 6:38). His entire earthly life was a vow of dedication to the Father fulfilled without defection.
27.3: An allusion: the valuation of a male person between 20-60 years at 50 shekels of silver. The temple's system of monetary valuation for dedicated persons points toward the ultimate valuation. Thirty pieces of silver — the price for which Judas sold Jesus (Matt 26:15) — is the slave's legal price (Exod 21:32) and the price Zechariah received as wages (Zech 11:12-13). The one who was without price was priced by human assessors. Matthew interprets this as fulfillment: 'They took the thirty pieces of silver, the price of him on whom a price had been set' (Matt 27:9).
27.4: An allusion: the valuation of a woman 20-60 at 30 shekels. The graduated valuation system reflects the practical economics of temple service (men performed more labor-intensive tasks) without assigning moral worth. In Christ all are redeemed at the same price — his own blood: 'You were bought with a price' (1 Cor 6:20; 7:23). The costly, uniform price of redemption overrides the graduated valuations of the dedication system.
27.5: An allusion: the valuations for persons 5-20 years (male 20, female 10 shekels). The age-graduated system acknowledges different capacities for service. Christ receives all ages: 'Let the children come to me, and do not hinder them, for to such belongs the kingdom of heaven' (Matt 19:14). His redemption is not conditioned on capacity or age — all are equally valuable to the one who gave everything.
27.6: An allusion: persons 1 month to 5 years — even infants have a valuation in the dedication system. The very young are not outside the covenant's scope. Christ's own dedication to YHWH occurred at 40 days (Luke 2:22-24) — as an infant, he was brought to the temple under the system of Lev 12 and 27. The first temple presentation of the one who would replace the temple is embedded in this dedication law.
27.7: An allusion: persons 60 and above (male 15, female 10 shekels). The elderly receive a lower valuation in the labor-capacity system, but Christ honors the elderly: 'Honor your father and mother' (Matt 15:4). Simeon and Anna, both elderly, were the ones who recognized the infant Jesus in the temple (Luke 2:25-38) — the aged witnesses to the one who transcends the value-hierarchy of the dedication law.
27.8: An allusion: 'But if someone is too poor to pay the valuation, then he shall be presented before the priest, and the priest shall value him; the priest shall value him according to what the vower can afford.' The graduated mercy of the system — no one is excluded from dedication by poverty. Christ's redemption is available to all regardless of economic status: 'The Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he has anointed me to proclaim good news to the poor' (Luke 4:18). 'Come, everyone who thirsts, come to the waters; and he who has no money, come' (Isa 55:1).
27.9: An allusion: a dedicated animal 'shall be holy to YHWH' — it cannot be exchanged or substituted. Once given to YHWH, the animal is in a category of its own. Christ's sacrifice was a once-for-all event that cannot be replicated or exchanged: 'Nor was it to offer himself repeatedly, as the high priest enters the holy places every year with blood not his own, for then he would have had to suffer repeatedly since the foundation of the world. But as it is, he has appeared once for all at the end of the ages to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself' (Heb 9:25-26).
27.10: An allusion: 'He shall not exchange it or make a substitute for it, good for bad or bad for good; and if he does in fact substitute one animal for another, both it and the substitute shall be holy.' The non-exchangeability of the dedicated animal. Christ's sacrifice admits no substitute: there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved (Acts 4:12). The uniqueness and non-substitutability of the Levitical dedication points to the unrepeatable uniqueness of the cross.
27.11: An allusion: an unclean animal that cannot be offered may be brought before the priest for valuation and redemption. The system provides for the redemption of what cannot directly serve as an offering. Christ redeems what is 'unclean' — what cannot by itself approach YHWH: 'While we were still sinners, Christ died for us' (Rom 5:8). He does not require his people to be clean before he redeems them; he redeems the unclean.
27.12: An allusion: the priest values the unclean animal 'whether good or bad; as the priest values it, so it shall be.' The priestly assessment determines the redemption price. Christ as the great High Priest who both assesses and pays the redemption price himself — the unique situation where the priest is also the payment (Heb 9:11-14).
27.13: An allusion: if the owner wishes to redeem the dedicated unclean animal, he adds a fifth to the valuation. The 20% surcharge recurs throughout Leviticus for inadvertent violations and personal redemptions (Lev 5:16; 6:5; 22:14; 27:15, 19, 31). The surcharge principle acknowledges that removing something from the holy sphere carries a cost beyond the mere assessed value. Christ's redemption cost not a surcharge but everything: 'You were bought with a price' — the price being not silver and gold but 'the precious blood of Christ' (1 Pet 1:18-19).
27.14: An allusion: a house dedicated to YHWH is assessed by the priest. The household sanctuary dimension of the dedication law. Christ as the one who makes every home a potential temple: 'If anyone loves me, he will keep my word, and my Father will love him, and we will come to him and make our home with him' (John 14:23). The divine presence that the dedicated house pointed toward comes in the new covenant to dwell in every believer's heart.
27.15: An allusion: the owner who redeems his dedicated house adds a fifth. The redemption cost more than the original. This anticipates what became true in the incarnation: the cost of reclaiming what was lost was higher than the original gift. Creation was given freely; redemption cost the Son's blood. Paul: 'He who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all, how will he not also with him graciously give us all things?' (Rom 8:32).
27.16: An allusion: the dedicated field's valuation is based on the crop yield and the remaining years until Jubilee. The Jubilee year is the theological backdrop for this whole section — the 50-year cycle when debts were cancelled and lands returned to original owners. Christ inaugurated the true Jubilee: 'The Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he has anointed me to proclaim good news to the poor... to proclaim the year of the Lord's favor' (Luke 4:18-19, citing Isa 61:1-2). The 'year of the Lord's favor' is the Jubilee vocabulary applied to the Messianic age.
27.17: An allusion: a field dedicated from the Jubilee year gets the full valuation. The proximity to Jubilee determines the value — the closer to the year of release, the higher the value. In Christ the Jubilee has arrived permanently: the release is not 50 years hence but already present. 'Now is the acceptable time; behold, now is the day of salvation' (2 Cor 6:2). The Levitical field-valuation tied to temporal Jubilee points to the timeless Jubilee of redemption.
27.18: An allusion: a field dedicated after the Jubilee year is valued proportionally to the remaining years. The diminishing value as Jubilee recedes mirrors the way the old covenant system was always running down toward its fulfillment-point. Each year further from the last Jubilee meant more years of servitude remaining. Christ's coming was the final Jubilee that need never run down: his release is permanent.
27.19: An allusion: the owner who redeems his dedicated field adds a fifth to the valuation. The redemption surcharge applies here as in the house (v.15). The costliness of redemption is a consistent theme: bringing something back from the divine sphere into ordinary use always costs more than it was originally worth. This anticipates the costliness of Christ's work — redemption from YHWH's ownership back to human possession required the supreme payment.
27.20: An allusion: if the field is sold to another person before being redeemed, it cannot be redeemed. The point of no return in the human system. Christ's redemption knows no such limit: 'And they are justified by his grace as a gift, through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus' (Rom 3:24). No human debt-situation is so hopelessly transferred that Christ cannot redeem — he has already paid the price for all who will come to him.
27.21: An allusion: a field that goes unredeemed at Jubilee becomes cherem (devoted, irrevocably consecrated) to YHWH — priestly property. The cherem category appears here as the ultimate form of divine ownership. Christ, in his resurrection, entered fully into divine possession — 'he was raised from the dead and will never die again; death no longer has dominion over him' (Rom 6:9). What is given entirely to YHWH and not redeemed becomes permanently his; Christ's resurrection body belongs permanently to the divine life.
27.22: An allusion: a purchased field (not ancestral) dedicated to YHWH gets its own valuation based on the Jubilee. The non-ancestral field dedicated by its purchaser — this is the situation of Gentiles brought into the covenant through Christ. The Gentiles are not the ancestral inheritance of the covenant people, but they have been 'purchased' (1 Cor 6:20; Rev 5:9) and dedicated to YHWH through Christ. Even the field that was not originally 'yours' can be dedicated to YHWH.
27.23: An allusion: the priest calculates the valuation up to the Jubilee year, and the dedicated person pays that sum as holy to YHWH. The precise accounting of the dedication system contrasts with the comprehensive sweep of Christ's redemption: 'He gave himself as a ransom for all' (1 Tim 2:6). No ledger-calculation is required in the new covenant; the debt was paid in full at the cross.
27.24: An allusion: at Jubilee, the field returns to its original owner. The Jubilee-return to original ownership points to the eschatological restoration: all of creation returning to its Creator's full possession. 'The creation itself will be set free from its bondage to corruption and obtain the freedom of the glory of the children of God' (Rom 8:21). The Jubilee's land-return is the natural type of the new creation's total restoration.
27.25: An allusion: 'Every valuation shall be according to the shekel of the sanctuary, twenty gerahs to the shekel.' The sanctuary shekel is the standard — the holy measure defines value. Christ as the standard by which all spiritual worth is measured: 'In him the whole fullness of deity dwells bodily' (Col 2:9). Every spiritual valuation that matters is calibrated to the one who is the fullness of God, the holy standard made flesh.
27.26: A direct type: 'But a firstborn of animals, which as a firstborn belongs to YHWH, no man may dedicate; whether ox or sheep, it is YHWH's.' The firstborn belongs to YHWH by right — it needs no special dedication because it is already his. Christ as 'the firstborn of all creation' (Col 1:15) and 'the firstborn from the dead' (Col 1:18; Rev 1:5): he belongs to YHWH as no creature does, not by dedication but by nature. The firstborn law of Leviticus points to the eternal Son who is the Father's own, the firstborn who precedes and exceeds every created firstborn.
27.27: An allusion: an unclean animal's firstborn may be redeemed at valuation plus a fifth, or sold. The unclean-firstborn redemption is what Christ accomplishes for humanity: 'You were bought with a price' (1 Cor 7:23). Humanity, the 'firstborn' of the image-bearing creatures (Gen 1:26-28), had become unclean through the fall. Christ redeems the unclean firstborn at the ultimate price — not a fifth surcharge but his own life.
27.28: An allusion: 'But no devoted thing (cherem) that a man devotes to YHWH, of anything that he has, whether man or beast or inherited field, shall be sold or redeemed; every devoted thing is most holy to YHWH.' The cherem — the irrevocably devoted — cannot be redeemed. This is the most absolute category in the dedication system. Yet Christ crosses even this boundary: what was given over to destruction (the condemned, the totally cursed) is what he came to redeem (Gal 3:13: 'redeemed us from the curse of the law'). No depth of devotion-to-destruction is beyond his reach.
27.29: An allusion: 'No one devoted (cherem) who is to be devoted from mankind shall be ransomed; he shall surely be put to death.' The cherem person receives the death penalty — no ransom is possible. Christ took this unredeemable category upon himself: 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us' (Gal 3:13). He entered the unredeemable category, bore the death it required, and in doing so ransomed those who were under condemnation from which no human ransom was possible.
27.30: An allusion: 'Every tithe of the land, whether of the seed of the land or of the fruit of the trees, is YHWH's; it is holy to YHWH.' The tithe principle — the tenth belongs to YHWH, signifying that all belongs to him. Christ as the true firstfruits and tithe: 'Christ has been raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep' (1 Cor 15:20). He is the first tenth given to YHWH, guaranteeing that the whole harvest follows.
27.31: An allusion: if a person redeems any of their tithe, they must add a fifth. The surcharge for reclaiming what was dedicated to YHWH — a pattern consistent throughout ch27. Giving to YHWH and then taking back costs more than simply giving. The principle of not drawing back from dedication to God: 'No one who puts his hand to the plow and looks back is fit for the kingdom of God' (Luke 9:62).
27.32: An allusion: 'And every tithe of herds and flocks, every tenth animal of all that pass under the herdsman's staff, shall be holy to YHWH.' The tithe of animals counted by the herdsman — every tenth. The Good Shepherd image: Christ is the shepherd who counts each sheep (Matt 18:12-13; Luke 15:4-7). In a striking reversal, the one who owns all the flocks does not demand the tenth — he gives himself for every last sheep, including the lost one that is not yet in the fold.
27.33: An allusion: 'One shall not differentiate between good and bad, neither shall he make a substitute for it; and if he does substitute for it, then both it and the substitute shall be holy; it shall not be redeemed.' The absolute non-substitutability of the tithe. Christ's death as the non-substitutable sacrifice: 'There is salvation in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved' (Acts 4:12). No substitution is possible in the economy of salvation — it is Christ or nothing.
27.34: A direct fulfillment: 'These are the commandments that YHWH commanded Moses for the people of Israel on Mount Sinai.' The closing verse of Leviticus — the entire book, with all its sacrifice regulations, purity laws, festival calendar, holiness code, blessings and curses, and dedication laws, is summarized in one sentence: received on Sinai, mediated by Moses, intended for Israel. Christ fulfills each element: he is the sacrifice (Heb 9-10), the purification (Heb 9:14), the festival's fulfillment (1 Cor 5:7; 15:23; Acts 2), the Holiness Code's embodiment ('be holy for I am holy,' 1 Pet 1:16), the one who bore the curse (Gal 3:13) and secured the blessing (Gal 3:14), and the one fully dedicated to YHWH (John 17:19). 'The law was given through Moses; grace and truth came through Jesus Christ' (John 1:17).

20.1: A literary frame: YHWH addresses Moses with the penalty-provisions for the Holiness Code violations — the sanctions that enforce the statutes of chapter 19. The chapter opens with Molech-worship, the worst apostasy (child sacrifice). The escalating severity of the penalties (death, cutting off, childlessness) mirrors the gravity of covenant violation. Christ came not to set aside these moral boundaries but to fulfill the covenant demands himself: 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us' (Gal 3:13). The penalties of Leviticus 20 are the shadow of the curse Christ bore.
20.2: A shadow: 'Any person — whether Israelite or foreigner... who gives any of his children to Molech shall surely be put to death.' Molech-worship demanded child sacrifice — the darkest of ancient Near Eastern practices, offering a child's life to secure divine favor. The antithesis is the gospel: God does not demand the sacrifice of children — he gives his own Son: 'For God so loved the world, that he gave his only Son' (John 3:16). The Molech-worshipper sacrifices his child to gain life; God sacrifices his Son to give life to others. The law's harshest penalty guards the boundary that the gospel crosses from the opposite side.
20.3: A shadow: 'I will set my face against that man and will cut him off from among his people, because he has given one of his children to Molech, so as to make my sanctuary unclean and to profane my holy name.' The divine judgment ('I will set my face against') is the language of covenant wrath. Christ, bearing the covenant curse at the cross, faces the precise opposite: God does not set his face against Christ in permanent rejection but vindicates him in the resurrection. 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' (Matt 27:46) is the temporary moment of forsakenness that secures the permanent 'I will never leave you nor forsake you' (Heb 13:5) for those united to Christ.
20.4: A shadow: 'If the people of the land close their eyes to what that man does when he gives his children to Molech, and do not put him to death, then I will set my face against that man and his family.' The community bears responsibility for enforcing covenant standards — silence in the face of apostasy is complicity. Christ confronts this in his prophetic ministry: 'Woe to you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites! For you shut the kingdom of heaven in people's faces' (Matt 23:13). The church is called not to 'close its eyes' to covenant violation but to speak the truth in love (Eph 4:15), as Christ did without flinching.
20.5: A shadow: 'I will set my face against that man and his family and will cut off from among their people him and all who follow him in whoring after Molech.' The judgment extends to those who follow apostasy, not merely the instigator. Paul warns: 'Do not be deceived: bad company ruins good morals' (1 Cor 15:33). Christ separates the gathered from the scattered: 'Whoever is not with me is against me, and whoever does not gather with me scatters' (Matt 12:30). The cutting-off judgment points to the final separation Christ describes: 'I never knew you; depart from me, you workers of lawlessness' (Matt 7:23).
20.6: A shadow: 'If a person turns to mediums and necromancers, whoring after them, I will set my face against that person and will cut him off from among his people.' The consulting of the dead for guidance is the spiritual adultery that the law calls 'whoring' (zinnah). Christ is the living Lord who renders all necromancy obsolete: 'I am the first and the last, and the living one. I died, and behold I am alive forevermore, and I have the keys of Death and Hades' (Rev 1:17-18). The one who holds the keys of death is the only legitimate guide through death — seeking the dead for guidance is unnecessary when the risen Christ speaks by his Spirit.
20.7: A direct quotation type: 'Consecrate yourselves, therefore, and be holy, for I am YHWH your God.' Peter quotes this verse alongside Lev 19:2 in 1 Pet 1:15-16. The consecration-call is fulfilled in Christ's high-priestly prayer: 'Sanctify them in the truth; your word is truth. As you sent me into the world, so I have sent them into the world. And for their sake I consecrate myself, that they also may be sanctified in truth' (John 17:17-19). Christ consecrates himself so that his people may be consecrated — the source of all holiness is not human effort but union with the Holy One who has set himself apart for our sake.
20.8: A direct revelation: 'Keep my statutes and do them; I am YHWH who sanctifies you.' The divine self-identification as the one who sanctifies — YHWH mekadishchem, 'YHWH your sanctifier' — is one of the most important covenant titles in Leviticus. Paul applies the sanctification title to Christ: 'Jesus Christ, who became to us wisdom from God, righteousness and sanctification and redemption' (1 Cor 1:30). Christ is YHWH-who-sanctifies in person — the eternal 'I am YHWH' has a name: Jesus, who sanctifies 'those who draw near to God through him' (Heb 7:25).
20.9: A shadow: 'For anyone who curses his father or his mother shall surely be put to death.' The fifth commandment carries a death penalty for its violation at the extreme — the covenantal severity mirrors the seriousness of the parent-honor obligation. Christ himself bore 'the curse of the law' (Gal 3:13) in his death — not for his own violation (he fulfilled all the commandments) but for ours. The severity of Lev 20:9 is the legal backdrop for the NT's theology of Christ as the substitute who bore the death-penalty on behalf of covenant-breakers. 'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us — for it is written, Cursed is everyone who is hanged on a tree' (Gal 3:13).
20.10: A shadow: 'If a man commits adultery with the wife of his neighbor, both the adulterer and the adulteress shall surely be put to death.' When the scribes and Pharisees brought a woman caught in adultery to Jesus (John 8:3-11), they invoked Lev 20:10 — the law requiring death. Jesus's response — 'Let him who is without sin among you be the first to throw a stone' (John 8:7) — does not abolish the law but exposes that no accuser can stand before it either. He then forgives: 'Neither do I condemn you; go, and from now on sin no more.' Christ fulfills the law's demand by bearing it himself, and transforms its enforcement from condemnation to the call to repentance.
20.11: A shadow: incest (father's wife) carries the death penalty — 'their blood is upon them.' The escalating sexual holiness laws define the purity of the covenant community. Paul confronts a case in Corinth (1 Cor 5:1-5) — a man living with his father's wife — and quotes Lev 20:11 implicitly by demanding severe discipline: 'hand this man over to Satan for the destruction of the flesh, so that his spirit may be saved in the day of the Lord.' The church, as the new Israel, maintains the covenant purity boundaries, but now the goal is restoration, not merely punishment — the discipline serves the ultimate hope of redemption.
20.12: A shadow: 'If a man lies with his daughter-in-law, both of them shall surely be put to death.' The death penalties for incest establish the absolute boundaries of the family covenant-structure. Christ expands the family covenant while maintaining its purity: 'Whoever does the will of God, he is my brother and sister and mother' (Mark 3:35). The kingdom family is constituted by covenant fidelity to God, not natural descent — and it maintains, in the Spirit, the same purity that the Levitical law mandated externally.
20.13: A shadow: the death penalty for male homosexual intercourse is one of the Levitical boundaries Paul invokes in Romans 1:24-27 as evidence of the suppression of created order. Paul's point in Romans 1 is that the escalating moral disorder described there — including the abandonment of natural relations — is God's judgment (giving them over) upon a world that refuses to honor God. Christ's redemption reverses this disorder: 'such were some of you. But you were washed, you were sanctified, you were justified in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and by the Spirit of our God' (1 Cor 6:11). The law names the disorder; the gospel names the transformation.
20.14: A shadow: the prohibition of taking both a woman and her mother, with a death by fire. The sexual ethics of Leviticus 20 protect the integrity of the family unit — the basic building block of the covenant community. Christ presents himself as the one who ultimately unifies the family in a deeper sense: 'everyone who has left houses or brothers or sisters or father or mother or children or lands, for my name's sake, will receive a hundredfold and will inherit eternal life' (Matt 19:29). The kingdom family supersedes the biological family without abolishing it.
20.15: A shadow: bestiality — death for both man and animal. The death penalties in Leviticus 20 together constitute the covenant community's absolute boundary — the 'not this' by which the holy community is defined. Christ defines his people positively: 'my sheep hear my voice' (John 10:27). The laws of Leviticus 20 are the negative boundary-markers; the gospel is the positive call that brings people into the covenant community — called, washed, and renewed to reflect YHWH's holiness in positive obedience.
20.16: A shadow: the same death penalty applies to the woman in the case of Lev 20:16. The equal culpability of both parties reflects YHWH's impartial justice — no double standard. Christ's impartiality in judgment: 'God shows no partiality' (Rom 2:11). Paul applies this to the gospel: 'there is neither male nor female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus' (Gal 3:28). The covenant boundaries apply equally; the covenant grace is also equally available — 'there is no distinction: for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God, and are justified by his grace as a gift' (Rom 3:22-24).
20.17: A shadow: incest with a sister — 'a disgrace, and they shall be cut off in the sight of the children of their people.' The concept of 'cutting off' (karet) from the people is the covenant's internal exile — the offender is separated from YHWH's community and its blessings. Christ reverses karet by suffering the ultimate cutting off — 'he was cut off from the land of the living, stricken for the transgression of my people' (Isa 53:8). The exile of the covenant-breaker is borne by the covenant-keeper, so that those who deserve cutting off may be grafted in (Rom 11:17-24).
20.18: A shadow: the prohibition of intercourse during menstruation. The purity laws create a structure of separation and reunion — the monthly cycle of distance and return mirrors the broader OT pattern of exile and restoration, judgment and reconciliation. Christ abolishes the separation: the woman with the flow of blood who touched the hem of his garment (Mark 5:25-34) — ritually unclean, excluded from full temple access — is healed instantly, and Jesus calls her 'daughter.' The one who made her unclean touches him and makes him, as it were, responsible for her condition; instead, his holiness flows out and cleanses her.
20.19: A shadow: the prohibitions against intercourse with an aunt. The increasingly specific incest prohibitions in Leviticus 20 enumerate the covenant family's integrity. These laws gave Israel's family structure its distinctive covenant purity, setting it apart from surrounding cultures. The church inherits a transformed version of this family purity — not biological relations but spiritual ones. The community that is 'built up as a spiritual house' (1 Pet 2:5) maintains its integrity through Christ, 'the cornerstone' (1 Pet 2:6) who defines the shape of the covenant household.
20.20: A shadow: 'If a man lies with his uncle's wife, he has uncovered his uncle's nakedness; they shall bear their sin; they shall die childless.' The consequence of 'dying childless' resonates with the Abrahamic covenant: the promise was carried through offspring, and childlessness was the covenant curse. Christ reverses the spiritual childlessness of sin: 'I will give him... an everlasting name that shall not be cut off' (Isa 56:5). Through Christ, those who were spiritually cut off from the covenant become children of God: 'to all who received him, who believed in his name, he gave the right to become children of God' (John 1:12).
20.21: A shadow: 'If a man takes his brother's wife, it is impurity.' John the Baptist rebuked Herod for exactly this violation — 'It is not lawful for you to have your brother's wife' (Mark 6:18). His fidelity to Lev 20:21 cost him his head (Mark 6:27). The Baptist's martyrdom for covenant-truth is the type of Christ's own death: both are killed by political-religious collusion for speaking uncomfortable truth. 'A servant is not greater than his master. If they persecuted me, they will also persecute you' (John 15:20). The prophet who dies for covenant fidelity anticipates the Prophet who dies for covenant fulfillment.
20.22: A shadow: 'You shall therefore keep all my statutes and all my rules and do them, that the land where I am bringing you to live may not vomit you out.' The land of Canaan is the type of the kingdom of God — and the kingdom can expel the unfaithful. Christ teaches this with the parable of the wedding garment (Matt 22:11-13): entry is by grace, but complacent covenant-violation leads to exclusion. 'Every branch in me that does not bear fruit he takes away' (John 15:2). The promised land's threat of vomiting up the disobedient points to the kingdom's eschatological seriousness.
20.23: A shadow: 'And you shall not walk in the customs of the nation that I am driving out before you, for they did all these things, and therefore I detested them.' The ethical distinctiveness of Israel from the surrounding nations is grounded in YHWH's own standard — not cultural preference but divine character. Christ calls his people to the same counter-cultural distinctiveness: 'You are the light of the world' (Matt 5:14); 'Do not be conformed to this world' (Rom 12:2). The holiness-distance from surrounding culture that Leviticus legislated is now the Spirit-formed character of the new covenant community.
20.24: A shadow: 'I am YHWH your God, who has separated you from the peoples... to be mine.' The election-separation formula — 'I have set you apart' — is the covenant's foundational claim on Israel. Paul applies it to the church: 'But you are a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people for his own possession' (1 Pet 2:9, quoting Exod 19:6; also Deut 7:6). Christ is the one through whom this election-separation reaches the Gentiles — 'you once were not a people, but now you are God's people' (1 Pet 2:10). The Lord who separated Israel from the nations has in Christ separated a new covenant people from every nation.
20.25: A shadow: 'You shall therefore separate the clean beast from the unclean, and the unclean bird from the clean.' The clean/unclean animal distinction, established in Lev 11, is here applied in the broader context of Israel's separation from the nations. Peter's vision in Acts 10:9-16 — 'What God has made clean, do not call common' — directly abrogates this distinction. The clean/unclean food law was the physical enactment of the Jew/Gentile distinction; when Christ declares 'all foods clean' (Mark 7:19), the ethnic-boundary significance of the law is abolished in principle. The Gentile mission flows directly from this fulfillment.
20.26: A direct revelation: 'You shall be holy to me, for I YHWH am holy and have separated you from the peoples, that you should be mine.' This is the fullest statement of the Holiness Code's purpose: holiness is not a legal achievement but a relational status — set apart to belong to YHWH. In Christ, the belonging is made explicit in its fullest form: 'you are not your own, for you were bought with a price' (1 Cor 6:19-20). The church belongs to Christ as Israel belonged to YHWH — called out (ekklesia), purchased, and set apart. The 'you should be mine' of Lev 20:26 echoes in the covenant formula of Revelation: 'They will be his people, and God himself will be with them as their God' (Rev 21:3).
20.27: A shadow: 'A man or a woman who is a medium or a necromancer shall surely be put to death.' The death penalty for false spiritual mediation closes the chapter — the law protects the exclusive channel of divine communication. Christ as the sole mediator makes all other mediation unnecessary and therefore illegitimate: 'there is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus, who gave himself as a ransom for all' (1 Tim 2:5-6). The finality of the death penalty for false mediation in Lev 20:27 corresponds to the NT's insistence that 'there is salvation in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved' (Acts 4:12).

21.1: A shadow: the law of priestly defilement opens the chapter — priests are held to a higher standard regarding corpse-contact than ordinary Israelites. The entire chapter is Leviticus' most detailed treatment of the Aaronic high-priesthood's unique holiness requirements. Hebrews reads Leviticus 21 as the detailed typological preparation for understanding Christ as the unique high priest: 'For it was indeed fitting that we should have such a high priest, holy, innocent, unstained, separated from sinners, and exalted above the heavens' (Heb 7:26). Every specific requirement of ch. 21 finds its antitype in Christ's perfect high-priestly holiness.
21.2: A shadow: the priest may defile himself for close relatives — mother, father, son, daughter, brother. Even the strict purity requirements have a provision for intimate grief — the priest is not forbidden all mourning, only inappropriate mourning. Christ himself wept at Lazarus's tomb (John 11:35) — the incarnate high priest was not immune to human grief. 'For we do not have a high priest who is unable to sympathize with our weaknesses, but one who in every respect has been tempted as we are, yet without sinning' (Heb 4:15). The Levitical priest's limited mourning-right points to Christ's genuine solidarity with human grief.
21.3: A shadow: the unmarried sister who has no husband may be mourned. The provision for the vulnerable dependent — the unmarried sister — within the priestly mourning-exception reflects the law's care for those without a husband's protection.
