Christ as the husband and protector of the vulnerable: 'Husbands, love your wives, as Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her' (Eph 5:25). The church as the unmarried sister who belongs to Christ's household — not yet married at the parousia but under his protection and heading toward the wedding feast of the Lamb (Rev 19:7-9).
21.4: A shadow: 'He shall not defile himself as a husband among his people and so profane himself.' The priest's distinction from ordinary mourning practices marks his unique consecration — his identity as a servant of YHWH overrides the ordinary claims of social custom. Christ's identity overrides every other claim: 'Who is my mother, and who are my brothers?... whoever does the will of my Father in heaven is my brother and sister and mother' (Matt 12:48-50). The high priest's singular consecration to YHWH's service is the type of Christ's total dedication to the Father's will: 'My food is to do the will of him who sent me and to accomplish his work' (John 4:34).
21.5: A shadow: 'They shall not make bald patches on their heads, nor shave off the edges of their beards, nor make any cuts on their body.' These are the pagan mourning-rites that the priests are explicitly forbidden — the body-marking rituals that treated death as the ultimate power. Christ transforms grief with hope: 'I am the resurrection and the life. Whoever believes in me, though he die, yet shall he live' (John 11:25). The priest who was forbidden to mark his body with pagan death-mourning is the type of the resurrection community — 'We do not want you to be uninformed... about those who are asleep, that you may not grieve as others do who have no hope' (1 Thess 4:13).
21.6: A direct revelation: 'They shall be holy to their God and not profane the name of their God. For they offer the LORD's food offerings, the bread of their God; therefore they shall be holy.' The priests are holy because they handle holy things — the bread/food offerings of YHWH. Christ identifies himself as 'the bread of God... who comes down from heaven and gives life to the world' (John 6:33) — he is not merely the one who presents the bread but is himself the bread. The Levitical priest offers bread to God; Christ offers himself as the bread from God. The sacrifice and the sacrificer are one in Christ.
21.7: A shadow: 'They shall not marry a prostitute or a woman who has been defiled, neither shall they marry a woman divorced from her husband.' The priestly marriage requirements protect the covenant purity of the priestly line. Christ claims the church as his pure bride: 'I betrothed you to one husband, to present you as a pure virgin to Christ' (2 Cor 11:2). The church has indeed been 'defiled' by sin before Christ — 'we too once lived in the passions of our flesh' (Eph 2:3) — yet Christ gives himself up for her 'so that he might present the church to himself in splendor, without spot or wrinkle' (Eph 5:27). The priestly marriage law is the type of Christ's transforming love for an impure bride he makes pure.
21.8: A shadow: 'You shall sanctify him, for he offers the bread of your God. He shall be holy to you, for I, the LORD, who sanctify you, am holy.' The community's responsibility to recognize and honor the priest's holiness — 'you shall sanctify him' — mirrors the church's calling to honor Christ as holy. Peter applies the verb-of-sanctifying to Christ himself: 'In your hearts honor Christ the Lord as holy' (1 Pet 3:15, the same root as hagiazō, to sanctify). YHWH who sanctifies Israel (Lev 20:8) is the same one who sanctifies Christ's people through him — 'both the one who makes people holy and those who are made holy are of the same family' (Heb 2:11).
21.9: A shadow: 'And the daughter of any priest, if she profanes herself by whoring, profanes her father; she shall be burned with fire.' The highest penalty for the priest's daughter who prostitutes herself reflects how the priest's family bears the priest's holiness. Christ's people — his 'children' in the family of God — are called to holiness that reflects the Father's holiness: 'As obedient children, do not be conformed to the passions of your former ignorance, but as he who called you is holy, you also be holy in all your conduct' (1 Pet 1:14-15). The honor of the family name is at stake in how Christ's people live.
21.10: A direct type: 'The priest who is chief among his brothers, on whose head the anointing oil was poured and who has been consecrated to wear the garments, shall not let the hair of his head hang loose nor tear his clothes.' The high priest's distinctive holiness — anointing oil poured on his head — is the precise type of Christ's anointing. Jesus at the Jordan: 'the heavens were opened to him, and he saw the Spirit of God descending like a dove and coming to rest on him' (Matt 3:16). Mashiach (anointed one) and Christos (anointed) are the same title. The Levitical high priest with oil on his head is the enacted preview of the Messiah with the Spirit upon him.
21.11: A shadow: 'He shall not go in to any dead bodies nor make himself unclean, even for his father or for his mother.' The high priest's absolute prohibition of corpse-contact distinguishes him from all other priests — not even the closest family bereavement can interrupt his sanctuary service. Yet Christ enters death voluntarily: 'he tasted death for everyone' (Heb 2:9). The Levitical high priest's extreme separation from death is the shadow of Christ's extreme conquest of death — he does not merely avoid the dead but descends into death and rises from it, 'having the keys of Death and Hades' (Rev 1:18). He is not unclean by death; death is undone by him.
21.12: A shadow: 'He shall not go out of the sanctuary, lest he profane the sanctuary of his God, for the consecration of the anointing oil of his God is on him.' The high priest's constant presence in the sanctuary — he does not leave the holy space — is the type of Christ's perpetual heavenly intercession. 'He always lives to make intercession for them' (Heb 7:25). The Levitical high priest who cannot leave the sanctuary provides the image; Christ as the risen high priest who 'passed through the heavens' (Heb 4:14) and now stands permanently in God's presence for his people is the reality. The consecration of the anointing oil demands constant sanctuary service; the consecration of the Spirit demands Christ's perpetual intercession.
21.13: A shadow: 'And he shall take a wife in her virginity.' The high priest's marriage requirement — virgin from his own people — is the type of Christ's claim on the church. Paul: 'I betrothed you to one husband, to present you as a pure virgin to Christ' (2 Cor 11:2). The church is the virgin bride, and Christ is the bridegroom who claims her exclusively. The high priest cannot marry a widow, divorced woman, or prostitute — only a virgin. The church's purity in Christ is not natural but restored: 'you were washed, you were sanctified, you were justified in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ' (1 Cor 6:11). The high priest's virgin-bride requirement points to Christ's transforming love that makes his bride virgin.
21.14: A shadow: 'A widow, or a divorced woman, or a woman who has been defiled, or a prostitute — these he shall not marry.' The list of disqualified women for high-priestly marriage is the negative by which the positive is defined: the high priest seeks a pure virgin. Christ 'loved the church and gave himself up for her, that he might sanctify her... so that he might present the church to himself in splendor, without spot or wrinkle or any such thing, that she might be holy and without blemish' (Eph 5:25-27). What the law required the high priest to marry (a pure virgin), Christ creates in his church by his atoning work — the bride who was defiled is made pure.
21.15: A shadow: 'that he may not profane his offspring among his people.' The high priest's marriage purity protects the holiness of the priestly line — the offspring must be fit to serve in the sanctuary. Christ's 'offspring' — 'his seed' (Isa 53:10) — are those who believe in him and are born again by the Spirit: 'You are a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation' (1 Pet 2:9). The new covenant priesthood is constituted by the purity of the high priest's own sacrifice — not the purity of his marriage but the perfection of his offering. Those born from his sacrifice are qualified to be a 'holy priesthood, to offer spiritual sacrifices acceptable to God through Jesus Christ' (1 Pet 2:5).
21.16: A literary frame: YHWH addresses Moses with the priestly physical-defect laws — the list of conditions that disqualify a priest from altar service. The chapter's second half enumerates the disqualifications in detail. By contrast, Christ is the perfect high priest who embodies every positive quality: 'For it was indeed fitting that we should have such a high priest, holy, innocent, unstained, separated from sinners, and exalted above the heavens' (Heb 7:26). The Levitical disqualification list paints in negative space the portrait of the one who has no defect, no blemish, no impairment — the perfect high priest.
21.17: A shadow: 'None of your offspring throughout their generations who has a blemish may approach to offer the bread of his God.' The blemish-prohibition for altar service is the type of the unblemished sacrifice required. Peter applies it directly to Christ's sacrifice: 'you were ransomed... with the precious blood of Christ, like that of a lamb without blemish or spot' (1 Pet 1:18-19). The priest without a blemish must offer the sacrifice; in Christ, the priest and the sacrifice are the same — the unblemished priest offers his own unblemished life. The Levitical law required the priest to be whole; Christ was the only truly whole person to ever live, and he offered himself.
21.18: A shadow: the list of disqualifying conditions — blind, lame, mutilated face, limb too long. These physical conditions, though they disqualify from altar service, do not exclude from the priestly family (v. 22). Christ both fulfills the altar-service qualification (no defect) and inverts the exclusion: 'the blind receive their sight and the lame walk' (Matt 11:5). Those very persons excluded from the sanctuary in Lev 21 come to Jesus, the true temple: 'And the blind and the lame came to him in the temple, and he healed them' (Matt 21:14). The one who alone qualifies for the true altar service also removes every disqualification from those who come to him.
21.19: A shadow: 'a man who has an injured foot or an injured hand' — excluded from altar service. Yet Christ's own risen body bears the marks of his wounds: 'Put your finger here, and see my hands' (John 20:27). The risen high priest carries the marks of his suffering into the heavenly sanctuary — not as disqualifying blemishes but as the everlasting evidence of his completed sacrifice. 'It was fitting that he, for whom and by whom all things exist, in bringing many sons to glory, should make the founder of their salvation perfect through suffering' (Heb 2:10). The wounds of Christ are the credentials of the perfect high priest.
21.20: A shadow: the hunchback, the dwarf, the man with a defect in his sight, the man with eczema or scabs, the man with crushed testicles — all excluded from altar service. The comprehensiveness of the disqualification list — no physical imperfection was overlooked — creates by negative definition the portrait of perfect physical wholeness. Christ the perfect offering: 'He had no form or majesty that we should look at him, and no beauty that we should desire him' (Isa 53:2) — his outward appearance did not commend him, yet he was inwardly perfect. The Levitical list focuses on outer wholeness; Christ's perfection is the inner moral and spiritual wholeness that matters before God.
21.21: A shadow: 'No man of the offspring of Aaron the priest who has a blemish shall come near to offer the LORD's food offerings; since he has a blemish, he shall not come near to offer the bread of his God.' The disqualification is absolute for the offering — no blemished man at the altar. Christ is the one who passes this test absolutely: his offering is accepted because he himself is without defect. 'For by a single offering he has perfected for all time those who are being sanctified' (Heb 10:14). The single unblemished offering accomplishes what the law's perfect-offering requirement was pointing toward — not repeated imperfect sacrifices but one perfect sacrifice, once for all.
21.22: A shadow: 'He may eat the bread of his God, both of the most holy and of the holy things, but he shall not go through the veil or approach the altar, because he has a blemish.' The blemished priest may eat the holy food but cannot approach the altar — partial inclusion, not total exclusion. Christ tears the veil (Matt 27:51) — the barrier that limited access is removed. Now all who come to Christ have full access, not partial: 'we have confidence to enter the holy places by the blood of Jesus, by the new and living way that he opened for us through the curtain' (Heb 10:19-20). What the blemished priest was denied — full sanctuary access — is now given to all through Christ's blood.
21.23: A shadow: 'that he may not profane my sanctuaries, for I am the LORD who sanctifies them.' The word 'sanctuaries' (plural: the altar and the inner sanctuary) are YHWH's own — he is their sanctifier, not the priests. Christ as the one who sanctifies both the people and the sanctuary: 'both he who sanctifies and those who are sanctified are all of one origin. That is why he is not ashamed to call them brothers' (Heb 2:11). The holy sanctuaries of Leviticus point toward the heavenly sanctuary where Christ 'entered, not into holy places made with hands... but into heaven itself, now to appear in the presence of God on our behalf' (Heb 9:24).
21.24: A summary: 'So Moses spoke to Aaron and to his sons and to all the people of Israel.' The teaching moment — Moses transmits the laws of priestly holiness to Aaron, his sons, and all Israel. Christ as the teacher who surpasses Moses: 'The law was given through Moses; grace and truth came through Jesus Christ' (John 1:17). Moses mediates the law of priestly qualification; Christ himself is the qualification. 'For the law appoints men in their weakness as high priests, but the word of the oath, which came later than the law, appoints a Son who has been made perfect forever' (Heb 7:28). The Levitical teaching points to the living Teacher who embodies all it requires.
